normal expectations of a cross-functional engineering team
(branching, code review, integration testing, deployment);
(3) carry the work outwards beyond the camp's required scope
if I had the energy — which I did.

**Impact delivered:** **Algorithm curriculum — 81 problems, fully written up.**
Solved every problem in ByteDance's MarsCode Youth Training
Camp Juejin algorithm track and published them as a public
teaching repository at *juejin-algorithm-practice* (66
commits, 100% solo). Each problem ships with a per-problem
README (problem statement, examples, solution approach,
complexity analysis) and a tested Python implementation.
Topics span arrays/strings, dynamic programming, greedy
algorithms, math and number theory, binary search, graphs and
trees, bit manipulation, sorting and searching, simulation,
and encoding/decoding — the breadth a Chinese backend
engineer is expected to demonstrate at interview.

**Team 115 capstone — Douyin Mall backend.** Shipped backend
features on the team's Spring Boot 3 e-commerce platform
covering user management, product management, shopping cart,
order processing, and an AI customer-service module. The
repo carries 174 commits from 8 contributors; **my 45
commits placed me as the #2-most-prolific committer on the
team** (~26% of the codebase's commit history), behind only
the team lead. Stack: Java 17 + Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis +
MySQL + Redis + Alibaba Cloud services + Docker, with the
kind of microservice-style internal layering the camp's
evaluation rubric expects.

**Frontend supplement — built solo to integrate against the
team backend.** Authored a separate Vue 3 + Vite + TypeScript
+ Pinia + Vue Router frontend (`douyin-mall-frontend`, 15
commits, 100% mine) with end-to-end Cypress and Vitest unit
coverage, so the team's backend had a clean live-integration
target. This wasn't required by the camp; I wrote it to close
the loop on the system as a working product.

**Two open-source teaching templates distilled from the
capstone.** The camp's full-stack architecture had enough
reusable shape to be worth packaging as starter kits for the
next cohort of learners, so I built and published two
clean-architecture skeletons:

- **`douyin-mall-go-template`** (a.k.a. "TikTok Shop Go
  Template") — Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis backend with
  standard `cmd/`, `internal/`, `api/`, `pkg/`, `configs/`,
  `docs/`, `frontend/`, `logs/` layout, JWT auth, RESTful
  APIs, and a paired React frontend — 27 commits, 100%
  solo, bilingual EN + 简体中文 README. This is the same
  repository now featured as one of the eight projects on
  my GitHub Open Source primary table.
- **`douyin-mall-java-template`** — the Spring Boot + Maven
  counterpart with Maven wrapper, conventional `src/main` /
  `src/test` layout — 13 commits, 100% solo, bilingual
  README. Intended as the Java track's mirror of the Go
  template.

The five repositories together — algorithm track, capstone
backend, supplementary frontend, two architecture templates
— represent a deliberately overcomplete portfolio of camp
artefacts: most trainees deliver the algorithm submissions
and the team capstone; very few add a solo frontend and two
production-shape architecture templates on top.

**Technical highlights:** **Java / Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis + MySQL + Redis +
Alibaba Cloud** on the Team-115 capstone, which is the
canonical Chinese-internet backend stack. The architecture
is layered along the lines mainstream ByteDance projects
use — controller / service / mapper separation, Redis-
backed caching for hot product and cart paths, MyBatis for
explicit SQL control rather than JPA's abstraction, and
Alibaba Cloud services for OSS / SMS / message queue
integration. This is the stack a Chinese backend engineer
is expected to have shipped in production.

**Go + Gin clean-architecture template** in
`douyin-mall-go-template`. The directory shape (`cmd/`,
`internal/`, `pkg/`, `api/`, `configs/`, `docs/`, `logs/`,
`frontend/`) is the layout the Go community treats as
production-correct — separation between executable entry
points, internal packages, and reusable libraries; explicit
config and docs trees; logs as a deployment artefact. The
template ships JWT authentication, RESTful API conventions,
and a complete e-commerce domain model so that a learner
forking it has the shape of a real service rather than a
scaffolded stub.

**Vue 3 + Vite + Pinia + Vue Router + TypeScript** on the
frontend supplement, with a non-trivial testing pyramid
(Vitest for unit, Cypress for e2e via `start-server-and-
test`) — explicitly the configuration that lets you run
e2e tests both in CI (`vite preview` + `cypress run`) and
interactively (`vite dev` + `cypress open`).

**Algorithm fluency across ten topic areas** — the 81
problems on Juejin span the topic distribution Chinese
big-tech interview panels actually test, including the
weaker spots most candidates have (bit manipulation,
simulation, encoding/decoding) rather than just the Leetcode
favourites (arrays, DP). Each solution has explicit
complexity analysis, which is how the camp graded them.

**Bilingual technical documentation discipline** — every
repository I authored or led for the camp ships
side-by-side English and 简体中文 READMEs at production
quality. This is the documentation expectation inside a
Chinese megacorp with global products, and it's a habit I
carried out of the camp into every subsequent project.

**Team collaboration at the right scale** — being the #2
committer on an 8-person team taught the specific muscles
that solo projects don't: PR review etiquette, integration
conflict resolution, sharing test fixtures across modules,
and writing commit messages that another contributor will
read.
### Full Stack Developer & Lead Documenter (Lincoln University COMP693 Industry Placement) at CORDE (NZ infrastructure & civil construction)
[tier=secondary · recency=historical · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Period:** Jun 2024 -- Nov 2024
- **Location:** Canterbury, New Zealand
- **URL:** https://corde.nz/

**Impact:** #1-most-prolific contributor (42% of 539 commits) on CORDE's offline-first React Native field-operations app — still widely used today by CORDE's water/civil-infrastructure crews. Also owned 97.9% of the documentation track end-to-end.

**Company context:** **CORDE** is a Canterbury-based **New Zealand infrastructure
and civil-construction company** — 30+ years of operations,
roughly 164 staff (LinkedIn category 201–500), trading
nationally under the tagline *"Trusted Connections."* In
February 2022 three separate Canterbury entities were unified
under the CORDE brand. The company operates across three
specialist arms — **Civil Construction**, **Regional Asset
Maintenance** (open-space management; introduced autonomous
mowing tech to NZ via partnerships with Colbrook Industries
and RC Mowers USA), and **Water Infrastructure / three waters**
— across three Canterbury offices: head office at **85
Hoskyns Road, Rolleston**, plus **Timaru** (South Canterbury)
and **Rangiora** (North Canterbury). Recognised projects
include Te Whāriki Subdivision Stage 3, Windsorcare Village
Stage 1, and the Ellesmere-to-Pines Pipeline Project; awards
include **CCNZ Canterbury Contractor of the Year** and a
**Construction Excellence National Award**. CORDE recently
acquired Arnold Jensen Electrical (70+-year-old NZ electrical
specialist) to bring electrical capability in-house. This
is a substantial regional employer, not a small client.

The engagement was a **Lincoln University COMP693 industry-
placement capstone** — the practicum module of the Master of
Applied Computing programme — embedded directly inside CORDE
with weekly on-site meetings at their Canterbury offices,
distributed online work between meetings, and CORDE's own
engineering processes (Azure DevOps for project management,
Figma for design hand-off, Apifox + Swagger for API contract
testing) governing how the work shipped. Five months from
June 2024 kickoff to November 2024 delivery.

**Mission:** Solve a concrete operational problem inside a working
infrastructure firm: CORDE's field crews — water-
infrastructure technicians, civil-construction site teams,
regional-asset-maintenance operators — routinely work in
rural and semi-rural Canterbury where mobile network coverage
is unreliable. The company's central operations platform
(referred to as "Workbench" in the API contracts) is web-
based and assumes connectivity. The mobile app's job was to
make the field worker's tablet the **system of record for
the moment of work**, capture text and photos and GPS
simultaneously, hold it locally with integrity guarantees,
and synchronise back to Workbench cleanly when connectivity
returned — including handling the conflict cases that real
offline-first systems have to handle. This is a real
productivity problem worth real engineering for a 164-staff
infrastructure operator.

**My mandate:** Full-stack developer on a two-active-developer Lincoln
University team (myself plus Luke Shi / Zhenzhou Shi),
reporting directly to **Barry Clark** on CORDE's side as
the business sponsor and to **Kullum Ladley** as CORDE's
technical liaison and Workbench API specification author.
Mandate covered: front-end implementation across the entire
React Native + Native Base UI layer, database design (ERD
work co-designed with CORDE's existing database slices),
business-logic diagrams and architectural documentation,
weekly sprint-meeting question sets sent to CORDE, stage-
development presentation decks, live app demos and screen
recordings, and APK packaging for weekly CORDE reviews.
Documentation lead was an explicit role inside the team —
the docs repository carries a top-level folder literally
titled "*Chan Meng's Documentation Contribution Collection*"
with separate sub-folders for each of those mandates.

**Impact delivered:** **Shipped a production-quality React Native offline-data-
collection mobile app** for CORDE's field-operations
workflow. The codebase carries **539 commits across three
contributors**; **my 227 commits (combining the Chan Meng
and Chan handles) make me the #1-most-prolific contributor
— 42% of the entire commit history** — with Luke Shi (the
repo owner under his GitHub handle gentoo111) on 28.6% and
Xue Zheng on 25.8%. The app integrates with CORDE's
Workbench central operations platform via the API contract
documented across a sequence of **15 specifications
authored by Kullum Ladley** (the same Kullum named in every
API document's filename prefix `YYYYMMDD_Kullum_...`),
covering Workbench API calls, log creation, mobile-app
authentication, log extension fields, file-upload pipelines,
and group completion workflow — all captured under version
control as the API/ folder of the team's documentation
repository.

**Owned the documentation track end-to-end.** The team's
documentation repository (`team-corde`) has 143 commits;
**my 140 (97.9%) make me effectively the sole author.**
The repo contains: day-by-day weekly project work records
(the visible diary of the engagement across five months —
every UI library evaluation, every database-schema
iteration, every employer-email outcome, every offline
meeting decision), the project description and user-stories
archive, the full API documentation set from CORDE, and
seven separate sub-folders enumerating my specific
contributions — business logic diagrams, presentation
decks, database design, UI/UX and front-end implementation,
live demos and screen recordings, APK packaging, and
weekly sprint question sets.

**Practised the soft skills the capstone is designed to
teach.** Translated CORDE's abstract user stories into
concrete Azure DevOps work items; co-designed the database
schema by reconciling two team-developed plans against
CORDE's existing database slices into a unified version;
survived two UI library re-evaluations (Native Base →
React Native Elements → Native Base) and chose Native Base
for its Figma-component-library integration so design and
implementation stayed close; ran the demo cadence in front
of Barry Clark and the CORDE technical team every week
across five months; and made the team's GitHub one-branch
policy work even though it required disciplined sequencing
of two developers' parallel work.

**Kept the codebase modern after the engagement ended.**
Continued making targeted commits in subsequent years for
React Native version currency, Android 16 KB page-size
linker compliance, husky + lint-staged pre-commit hooks,
and dependency upgrades — keeping the repository a usable
portfolio piece rather than an archived snapshot of
mid-2024 React Native conventions.

**The application remains in active use today by CORDE's
field crews** for offline data collection — GPS-tagged
photos and structured form data captured on water-
infrastructure and civil-construction sites where mobile
coverage drops out, then syncing back when crews return
to coverage. The codebase-currency commits aren't archive
maintenance; they're keeping a production tool aligned
with the platform conventions of the year the crews are
actually running it on.

**Technical highlights:** **React Native 0.83 + TypeScript + Native Base** on the
client, deliberately chosen over Expo because the project's
offline-first requirements pushed against Expo's managed
workflow (and over React Native Elements because Native
Base shipped a Figma component library that let the team
do design-first then implement against the exact
components). The native-only mode also opened the door to
Android-native development where the offline pipeline
needed it.

**SQLite as the system of record for the moment of work.**
Local-first storage of text + photo + GPS coordinates +
timestamps captured in the field, with a synchronisation
pipeline that reconciles back to CORDE's Workbench central
database when connectivity returns. The sync model had to
handle the real conflict cases offline-first systems
actually face — duplicate-key collisions when a worker's
local IDs and the server's IDs both move forward, partial
sync where one log uploads but its attachments don't,
connectivity drop mid-batch, and the file-upload
sub-protocol covered separately in Kullum's API specifications.

**Heavy native-integration surface:** geolocation +
geolocation-service (foreground and background), camera-
roll, image-picker, document-picker, file-system, secure
keychain storage, native maps, react-native-permissions
for the Android permission flow, base64 encoding for the
on-the-wire payloads. The dependency list is twice as long
as a typical RN app because field-data-collection in
Canterbury's rural water-infrastructure context genuinely
touches that much of the device.

**API integration discipline.** Apifox for shared interface
testing across the team; Swagger as the contract source
from CORDE; a `test-sharepoint-connection.js` script as
evidence of a SharePoint-touching corner of CORDE's
document workflow; Azure DevOps as the work-item store
Barry Clark and the CORDE team worked from; per-week
question sets compiled into documents and reviewed before
each offline meeting so CORDE's time was used efficiently.

**The documentation repo is the artefact.** A second
repository (`team-corde`) ran in parallel as the project's
living memory — every weekly meeting decision, every
UI-library evaluation, every database-schema iteration,
every CORDE email exchange's outcome, captured in
version-controlled prose. This is unusually disciplined
documentation for a student-team engagement and is the
practice I carried into every subsequent role.

"Her Voice" podcast to amplify women's voices in tech. Bi-weekly

environments — coordinated through a dedicated volunteer liaison.

local audiences — covering traditional dance, music, and language.
The placement sharpened cross-cultural communication skills and

international settings. An early signal of the cross-Pacific
orientation that later anchored Chan's work between China and NZ.

## Flagship Projects

### 1. Google News MCP Server — Early-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 5 weeks after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 MCP launch) — PulseMCP "Top Pick", featured in Skywork AI deep-dive review, distributed across 15+ MCP catalogs
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, stdio transport, SerpAPI, axios, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666)
- **First commit:** 2024-12-30 — 35 days after MCP's Nov 25 2024 public launch
- **npm package:** @chanmeng666/google-news-server
- **Registry listings:** 15+ MCP catalogs (Smithery, mcp.so, Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers, MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, mcpservers.org, Archestra, serp.ai, mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub, MagicSlides)
- **PulseMCP badge:** Top Pick · 26.1k visitors · #1,105 global rank
- **Glama quality:** A (license) · 122 stars
- **Skywork review:** Dedicated Oct 2025 deep-dive article + AI-Engineer's Guide companion piece
- **Stars:** ~126 GitHub · 20 forks · listed on Smithery, Glama, PulseMCP (Top Pick), Archestra, mcp.so, Enterprise DNA, Augment Code, serp.ai
- **Forks:** 20
- **Commits:** 98 (Google News) + 71 (Google Jobs) = 169
- **Early MCP contrib:** Issue #76 (Nov 27 2024 — Windows/NVM workaround)
**Summary:** An early-ecosystem MCP server — first commit **Dec 30 2024, just
35 days after Anthropic's public launch of the Model Context
Protocol on Nov 25 2024**. Brings Google News search to AI
assistants like Claude Desktop, Cline, and Cursor through SerpAPI,
with automatic topical categorization (AI & Technology, Business,
Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and multi-language /
multi-region support. Carries the **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge**
with 26.1k estimated visitors and a #1,105 global popularity rank.
Featured in three independent deep-dive write-ups: Skywork AI's
*"Chan Meng's Google News MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Guide to
Real-Time News Integration"*, Skywork's *"Google News & Trends
MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Deep Dive"*, and AgentSkillsHub's
*"Install Guide & Security Review"*. Distributed across **15+
MCP catalogs and registries** — Smithery, mcp.so, Glama (A
license rating, 122 stars), PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers,
MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, mcpservers.org,
Archestra, serp.ai, mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub,
MagicSlides — and npm-published as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`. Paired with a companion early
server, `server-google-jobs` (first commit Dec 31 2024) —
together 169 solo commits across the two servers. Beyond shipping
code, I contributed early ecosystem troubleshooting via
[modelcontextprotocol/servers issue #76](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers/issues/76)
(opened Nov 27 2024) — the Windows + NVM PATH workaround
referenced in both server READMEs as primary troubleshooting
guidance.

**Impact:** Earliest-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 35 days after Anthropic's Nov 2024 MCP launch) — now listed across 15+ MCP catalogs, earned PulseMCP's 'Top Pick' badge and Glama's A-rating, used by AI assistants worldwide for current-events search.

**Problem:** Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol specification in
November 2024 as the open standard for connecting AI assistants
like Claude to external tools and data sources — but in the first
weeks after launch, the ecosystem had only a handful of reference
servers and no real way for AI assistants to search current news.
Generative engines (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity) were increasingly
being asked "what's happening with X today" — questions an LLM
cannot answer from training data alone. The gap was concrete:
Claude Desktop users wanted current-events search; the MCP server
catalog had nothing for Google News.

A second, related friction surfaced almost immediately: Windows
users running Node Version Manager couldn't get any npm-based MCP
server (filesystem, github, postgres, brave-search, google-maps,
etc.) to connect to Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution
through NVM's shim layer broke the desktop client's process
spawn. This blocked a large fraction of early Windows adopters
from using MCP at all.

**Audience:** AI assistants and the developers integrating them. Direct
consumers: Claude Desktop, Cline, Cursor's agent mode, Continue,
any MCP-compliant client running a system prompt that includes
news-search capability. Downstream consumers: end-users asking
their AI assistant about current events, journalists / analysts
using AI workflows to monitor coverage, developers building
research agents and content workflows. The multi-registry
distribution surface (10+ catalogs) was designed for *discovery*:
AI developers find MCP servers by browsing registries, not by
searching GitHub.

**Product logic:** A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening
with [topic] in [region] this week"; the assistant invokes the
MCP server's `google_news_search` tool with `query`, optional
`gl` (country code), and `hl` (language code) parameters; the
server relays the request to SerpAPI's `google_news` engine and
returns structured JSON; the server then post-processes the
results with regex-based topic categorization (AI & Technology,
Business, Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and formats
the output with source attribution, dates, snippets, and links
intact.

The companion `server-google-jobs` follows the same pattern with
a richer parameter set — `query` plus `location`, `posted_age`
(today / 3 days / week / month), `employment_type`
(FULLTIME / PARTTIME / CONTRACTOR / INTERN), `salary`, `radius`,
`hl`, `page`, `sort_by` — and ships with **full localization
for English, Simplified Chinese, Japanese, and Korean**
(30+ translated strings per locale).

Both servers run over MCP's `stdio` transport — the default
channel for desktop AI clients — and are installable in one
command via Smithery's CLI:
`npx -y @smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client claude`.

**Business logic:** Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The strategic
intent is **ecosystem positioning**: be the canonical Google
News / Google Jobs MCP server during the formative months of the
Model Context Protocol so the project compounds discovery for
adjacent work. Multi-registry distribution (Smithery, mcp.so,
Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers, mcpservers.org, MCP
Archive, MCP Server Finder, Playbooks, Archestra) makes the
project surface anywhere a developer looks for an MCP tool.
The README's structured JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata
and `<script type="text/llms.txt">` section take the same
approach to LLM-driven discovery that the rest of my work uses:
AI agents themselves should be able to read the server's
identity, parameters, and use cases without parsing prose.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
v1.0.0 (Google News) and v1.25.1 (Google Jobs — the newer SDK
pin reflects ongoing maintenance). ES module target, packaged
as a CLI binary (`google-news-server`, `google-jobs-server`)
exported in `package.json`. Transport is stdio — the default
channel for desktop AI clients, where the server runs as a
spawned subprocess of Claude Desktop or equivalent. HTTP is
handled with axios on the News server and node-fetch on the
Jobs server (deliberately leaner — no axios, no dotenv
overhead, environment read directly from `process.env`).

SerpAPI (paid tier required for News and Jobs query volume;
2 req/sec rate limit, 5 concurrent, 1 h cache) is the underlying
data plane. The servers add a thin
intelligence layer on top: query parameter validation,
categorization post-processing, multi-language formatting,
error handling with localized messages. Both READMEs are 320-335
lines and structured with badges, video demos, MseeP.ai
security badges, JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` schema, and an
explicit "AI Assistant Instructions & GEO Optimization"
collapsible section — the same GEO-aware authoring pattern I
use across all my work.

Beyond shipping code, the third technical contribution is
community: my **issue #76** (opened Nov 27 2024, three weeks
after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM `PATH`
resolution failure that blocked Claude Desktop from spawning
npm-based MCP servers. The fix — point Claude Desktop's config
at the absolute path of `node.exe` rather than relying on PATH
— is referenced as primary troubleshooting guidance in both my
own server READMEs and (per the README's own claim) became
guidance in the broader MCP ecosystem.

**Outcomes:** Both servers are production-deployed via npm and listed across
**15+ MCP discovery surfaces**, with independent third-party
validation that this is treated as portfolio-grade MCP work:

**Catalog distribution & quality signals (server-google-news,
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`):**
- **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge** + 26.1k estimated visitors,
  #1,105 global popularity rank
  ([listing](https://www.pulsemcp.com/servers/chanmeng666-google-news))
- **Glama A-grade license rating** + 122 stars
  ([listing](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/@ChanMeng666/server-google-news))
- Smithery one-command install
- Listed across mcp.so, Awesome MCP Servers (mcpservers.org),
  MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, Archestra, serp.ai,
  mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub, MagicSlides

**Catalog distribution (server-google-jobs,
`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`):**
- PulseMCP listing — 4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025
- Glama listed (A license rating)
- AI Toolhouse, mcp.so, AIBase, MagicSlides, serp.ly

**Independent media coverage:** three dedicated deep-dive
articles by third-party reviewers in 2025:
- Skywork AI: *"The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's Google Jobs
  MCP Server"* (Oct 27, 2025) — describes the project as *"one
  of the most elegant, production-ready solutions I've found
  for a very specific, very common problem"* and benchmarks it
  favourably against generic SerpAPI wrappers, custom scrapers,
  and manual API integrations
- Skywork AI: *"Chan Meng's Google News MCP Server: The AI
  Engineer's Guide to Real-Time News Integration"*
- Skywork AI: *"Google News & Trends MCP Server: The AI
  Engineer's Deep Dive"*
- AgentSkillsHub: *"Google News MCP Server — Install Guide &
  Security Review"*

**Combined engineering footprint:** 169 commits across the two
servers, both authored solo, actively maintained through
2025–26. Both serve as concrete artefacts for the
early-MCP-developer narrative — **first commits Dec 30 and
Dec 31 2024, within 5–6 weeks of MCP's Nov 25 2024 public
launch**. Issue #76 remains visible in the official
`modelcontextprotocol/servers` repository as institutional
memory of an early ecosystem contribution.

**My role:** Sole author, sole maintainer, sole responder to issues and PRs.
Both servers, both READMEs, both npm publishes, both registry
submissions, the issue #76 troubleshooting contribution.

The broader signal: these two servers + the early issue #76
contribution position me as an immediately-active MCP
ecosystem participant — not a theoretical early adopter, but
someone who shipped, distributed, documented, and supported
production MCP infrastructure within the protocol's first
month.
### 2. echook (Echo + Hook) — AI-operated hooks plugin for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only; grew from audio notifications into a context-window status line and richer session telemetry
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-10]
- **URL:** 
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/echook
- **Tech stack:** Python 3.6+, Claude Code Plugin SDK, Cursor IDE 3.2.16+ third-party hooks, Codex CLI hooks API, hooks.json native matcher routing, NDJSON structured logging (schema audio-hooks.v1), ElevenLabs TTS API, Slack / Discord / Teams / ntfy webhooks, PowerShell MediaPlayer (Windows), afplay (macOS), mpg123 / ffplay / paplay / aplay (Linux auto-detect), GitHub Actions CI (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9/3.12/3.13), Remotion 4 (promo video), React 19 (promo video), Suno (background music generation)
- **Commits (solo):** 150 (2025-11-01 → 2026-07-07, ~8 months); effectively single-author
- **GitHub stars:** ~76 (echook, renamed from claude-code-audio-hooks)
- **Version:** v6.3.3 (plugin `audio-hooks` on the chanmeng-audio-hooks marketplace)
- **Hook events:** 39 canonical events (6 on by default), mapped per harness: Claude Code 39 · Cursor 19 native / 8 via auto-bridge · Codex 10
- **Harnesses:** 3 (Claude Code · Cursor IDE · Codex CLI) from one canonical source, CI-enforced no-drift
- **Runtime:** Python 3.6+ standard library only (no third-party runtime deps); distributed via plugin marketplaces + source, not npm
- **CLI:** ~40 JSON-in/JSON-out subcommands; a `manifest` command emits the entire surface (subcommands, hooks, config keys, error codes) as machine-readable JSON so an agent can self-orient
- **Status line:** 29 configurable segments (context-window color bands, quota bars, model/branch/cost), auto-reflowing to terminal width
- **Channels:** audio · desktop toast · TTS (speaks the agent's actual final message) · webhook (Slack/Discord/Teams/ntfy, versioned payload)
- **Tests / CI:** 200+ unittest cases on a 3-OS × 3-Python matrix (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × 3.9/3.12/3.13) + build-drift gate
**Summary:** AI-operated audio notification system for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and
Codex CLI. The defining product idea: the **human never learns the
tool**. The user talks to their AI agent in plain English ("install
audio-hooks", "switch to chimes", "snooze for 30 minutes", "send alerts
to my Slack"), and the agent runs every shell command, reads every JSON
output, and reports back. Install reduces to **1 shell command + 2
natural-language prompts + 1 slash command = 4 things**; everything
after that is conversation. Ships 39 hook events, 2 audio themes,
rate-limit alerts (5h/7d quota windows), webhooks (Slack/Discord/Teams/
ntfy), TTS that speaks Claude's actual reply, and a 29-segment
context-window status line. Now on v6.3.3 with ~76 GitHub stars and
200+ unittest cases on Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9–3.13. A sister repo,
**echook-promo-video**, ships the 88-second 1080p product film built
entirely in code (Remotion + Claude Code + ElevenLabs + Suno).
Carved out as Chan's preserved IP in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter (May 2026).

**Impact:** AI-operated hook system for Claude Code / Cursor / Codex, driven entirely by natural language — 39 hook events across three harnesses from one source, a 29-segment context-window status line, audio/TTS/webhook channels, 200+ tests on triple-platform CI. ~76 stars, v6.3.3.

**Problem:** Developers running long-form AI coding workflows on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no way to *hear* what their agent
is doing — they alt-tab to check progress, miss rate-limit
warnings, and only discover stuck sessions on visual inspection.
Bolt-on notification tools exist but they all carry the same
anti-pattern: **the human is downstream of the CLI**. The user
has to read docs, memorise flags, copy commands, and re-learn the
same surface every time it changes. That model collapses the
moment a serious user starts rotating between Claude Code, Cursor,
and Codex — three editors, three configuration languages, three
sets of muscle memory. The mismatch isn't audio; it's the
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.

**Audience:** Software engineers operating Claude Code / Cursor IDE / Codex CLI
at the "AI-first" level — comfortable telling their coding agent
to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure infrastructure
in plain English rather than memorising subcommands. Also: power
users debugging long-running workflows where audio cues are faster
than visual polling; teams rolling out shared notification
defaults across multiple developers; researchers building on the
AI-first plugin contract as a reference implementation.

**Product logic:** echook inverts the relationship: **the human is upstream of
Claude Code, the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf.**
A one-line install prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — kicks off the entire flow. Claude
Code runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the only
manual slash command in the entire lifetime of the tool, because
Claude Code's reload has no CLI equivalent), and from that moment
forward every interaction is conversation. *"Switch to chimes."*
*"Snooze for 8 hours."* *"Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook
URL and test it."* *"Disable everything except stop and
permission_request."* The `/audio-hooks` skill parses intent,
invokes the right `audio-hooks` CLI subcommand, reads its JSON
output, and narrates the result. The user memorises **nothing**.
Cross-editor parity is part of the same contract: every prompt
works identically on Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI because they
all drive the same Python binary against the same JSON API.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no telemetry.
The business value is **build-in-public credibility**: echook is
the demonstration artifact for Chan's AI-first product philosophy
and shows up in every Engram-cohort interview, every recruiter
conversation, and the public README of every downstream client
project. The sustained release cadence (now v6.3.3), the
cross-platform CI matrix, and the 200+-test bridge-contract suite
make it visible production
engineering — not a weekend script. The Remotion promo video
sister repo extends the same proof: it shows Chan can ship not
just the product but the marketing artifact for the product, end
to end, programmatically, with zero Adobe-suite licensing or
third-party video production overhead.

**Technical approach:** AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` CLI ships a
stable JSON-only API with **~40 subcommands, no prompts, no
spinners**, versioned NDJSON event logging (schema
`audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and a **stable error
code enum** (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`, `WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`,
`DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, etc.) where each error carries a
`suggested_command` field so an agent can recover autonomously
without screen-scraping. Hook coverage is **39 events** spanning
session start/end, tool execution, permission requests, file
changes, worktree creation, elicitation, and rate-limit warnings
(5-hour and 7-day quota windows with configurable 80%/95%
thresholds; each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` fires exactly
once via debounce markers). Two audio themes: ElevenLabs
**Jessica** voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are declarative
via `config/audio_manifest.json` + the ElevenLabs generator.
Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy via
fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload — they never block the hook.
The status line ships **29 customisable segments** including
color-coded context-window and API-quota bars with absolute
token counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they enter it. Cross-editor architecture
is the deepest piece: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— **one source of truth, three editor targets**, with CI
enforcing in-sync via `build-plugin.sh --check`. The Cursor path
leverages Cursor 3.2.16+'s auto-bridge so 99% of users get
Cursor support for free; Cursor-only installs go through a
native `~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration with a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error guard that aborts if both paths would
be active. The Codex CLI path goes further: install handles
OpenAI's required `[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` feature flag
by **authoring a fresh `~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists**
and emitting machine-readable `next_steps` for the calling AI
agent to add the flag with its Edit tool when one does — we
never round-trip user TOML because that destroys comments and
formatting. **18 canonical events with no Codex equivalent
no-op cleanly under the codex invoker** with a debug NDJSON
event, never blocking the session. The whole bridge contract is
validated by 200+ unittest cases across Ubuntu/Windows/macOS ×
Python 3.9/3.12/3.13 in GitHub Actions CI.

**Outcomes:** ~76 GitHub stars, **150 commits** across the 5.x → 6.x line (now
v6.3.3), **200+ unittest cases**. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan 2026) shipped
the AI-first redesign — JSON CLI, `/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON
logging, plugin-native install; v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native
Codex CLI support with +33 bridge-contract tests; v5.2.1 (May
2026) executed the **echook rebrand** — a "door-only" GitHub
rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` → `echook` (because leading
with "Claude Code" became misleading once native Cursor and
Codex paths shipped) that preserved every existing install via
GitHub's repo redirect, kept the `audio-hooks` CLI name, the
`chanmeng-audio-hooks` marketplace name, and all state
directories unchanged. The Engram Architect Cohort Engagement
Letter (May 9, 2026) explicitly names
*claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's preserved IP — formal
recognition by a commercial agentic infrastructure firm that this is
portfolio-grade engineering, not a hobby script. The sister
repo **echook-promo-video** then closed the loop on the
AI-first thesis: an 88.7-second 1920×1080 @ 30fps product film
composed of 9 sections (Intro → Problem → Solution → Features →
Advanced → Demo → Ecosystem → Cross-Platform → CTA) connected
by `@remotion/transitions` (wipe/fade/slide) with a layered
audio mix (Suno-generated BGM with auto volume ducking + 8
ElevenLabs SFX cues + 3 ElevenLabs voiceover tracks) — built
entirely in React + Remotion + AI-generated audio, **zero
Adobe Premiere, zero After Effects, zero human voice actors**.
The whole video pipeline is reproducible: one
`npm run generate-audio` regenerates every voice and effect from
the ElevenLabs API; one `npm run render` produces the MP4.

**My role:** Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON API + NDJSON log + stable error codes +
`suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronisation
pipeline (canonical → plugin layout sync via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML-feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the 9-section narrative arc with Claude Code, built
the reusable visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal,
SoundWave, GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated
the Remotion `TransitionSeries`, wrote the ElevenLabs
audio-generation script, and tuned the volume-ducking
envelopes so BGM ducks during VO + demo playback. Maintains
both repos today through the 5.x line.
### 3. ArchLang — An original declarative language Chan invented that compiles .arch source into professional, dimensioned floor plans — 'Typst/LaTeX for architecture.' Zero-dependency, isomorphic TypeScript (hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser), errors returned as data not thrown, with architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a deterministic repair pass, four export formats (SVG/DXF/PDF/PNG), an agent-native CLI, full LSP + VS Code extension, and a client-side playground. Published to npm; the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-10]
- **URL:** https://archlang-playground.vercel.app
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/archlang
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript 5.7 (ESM, zero runtime dependencies), Hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser + expression evaluator, tsup (build), Biome 2.5 (lint/format), Vitest 2.1 + fast-check (property tests) + pixelmatch (visual regression), Optional lazy add-ons: @resvg/resvg-js (PNG), clipper2-wasm (geometry), pdfkit (PDF), LSP (hover/completion/rename/signature), VS Code extension (ChanMeng.archlang, TextMate grammar), Vite + CodeMirror 6 playground (100% client-side), VitePress docs, GitHub Action (renders ```arch fences to images)
- **Commits (solo):** 192 (2026-06-25 → 2026-07-10, ~2.5 weeks intensive); sole author
- **npm package:** @chanmeng666/archlang v1.12.1 (13 versioned feature branches, 10 ADRs)
- **Source:** 80 TypeScript files (~19.7k LOC) + 82 test files
- **Primitives:** 8 elements (wall · room · door · window · opening · furniture · dim · column) + fixture-symbol catalog
- **Export formats:** 4 — SVG + DXF (zero-dep) · PDF + PNG (lazily-loaded optional add-ons)
- **Diagnostics:** 74 distinct E_*/W_* codes, each carrying a byte span + a machine-applicable fix
- **Lint rules:** 8 architectural-soundness rule modules (reachability, door-swing collision, windowless bedroom, wet-room enclosure, circulation)
- **CLI:** ~20 subcommands, all with --json + deterministic exit codes (0 ok / 1 IO / 2 source / 3 usage)
- **Downstream:** the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas, which consumes frozen-API contracts (data-arch-id anchors, diffPlans()) verbatim
**Summary:** ArchLang is a parametric DSL for floor plans: walls, rooms, doors,
windows, fixtures, and dimensions — placed by exact millimetre
coordinates or relationally (right-of / below with alignment + gap) —
compile to deterministic SVG (also DXF / PDF / PNG). The same source
always produces the identical drawing, so "make the bedroom 1m wider"
is a one-number diff rather than a re-rolled raster. It ships
architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a
deterministic repair pass, an agent-native CLI (JSON output, structured
diagnostics), full LSP + a VS Code extension, and a 100%-client-side
browser playground — as zero-runtime-dependency isomorphic TypeScript.

**Impact:** An original declarative floor-plan language Chan invented — compiling to professional dimensioned plans (Typst/LaTeX for architecture). Zero-dependency isomorphic TypeScript with errors-as-data, soundness linting, and circulation modeling; the engine behind ArchCanvas.

**Problem:** Floor plans live in two unsatisfying states: hand-drawn in heavy CAD
tools, or generated as raster images. Both make the thing you most
want to do — a precise, parametric edit — expensive: widening a
bedroom means re-drawing, or re-rolling an image and hoping the rest
survives. Neither is authorable by an AI agent, because there is no
deterministic text representation an agent can write, verify, and
tweak. And an image never *knows* whether the plan it depicts is
actually habitable — whether a bathroom is reachable only through a
bedroom, whether a door swings into another door, whether a bedroom
has a window. ArchLang exists to make a floor plan a **program**:
exact, deterministic, diff-able, agent-authorable, and checkable for
architectural soundness.

**Audience:** Three audiences. Architects and self-builders who want plans that are
precise and reproducible rather than hand-drawn approximations;
design-tool builders who need a floor-plan engine they can embed
(ArchCanvas is the first); and AI agents that need to author and
verify plans without ever rendering an image — for which ArchLang
exposes its entire language in one ~2k-token spec page and returns a
plan "as facts" (rooms, areas, adjacency, door connections,
circulation) over `describe --json`.

**Product logic:** You write `.arch` source. The coordinate model is integer millimetres
with an SVG-convention origin; elements are placed either absolutely
(`room 4000x3000 at (0,0)`) or **relationally** — `right-of`,
`left-of`, `above`, `below` with `align` and `gap`, resolved by
deterministic topological arithmetic so the layout stays consistent
under edits. The language is genuinely programmable: `let` bindings,
pure value-functions, `for`/`if`/`while`, arrays, string
interpolation, and `import` + `component` for reuse — all expand-time
and pure (no runtime, clock, or I/O), so output is byte-for-byte
deterministic.

Compilation is a returned value, not a side effect: `compile()` hands
back `{ svg, errors, warnings, diagnostics, ast, scene }` and **never
throws** on a bad plan — every diagnostic carries a byte span and a
machine-applicable `fix`, and the SVG is simply left empty. On top of
the compiler sit four analysis surfaces: a **lint** pass over eight
architectural-soundness rules (reachability, door-swing collision,
windowless bedroom, unenclosed wet room, too-narrow door…); a
**circulation** analysis that models how a person actually walks the
plan on a clearance-eroded nav grid (per-room walk distance, narrowest
pinch, path circuitousness) with an optional route overlay; a
deterministic **repair** pass that nudges furniture out of walls and
door swings and emits a change log; and **describe**, which returns
the plan as structured facts. All of this is reachable from a
~20-subcommand CLI where every command supports `--json` and returns
deterministic exit codes, plus an LSP, a VS Code extension, four
themes, accessibility annotations, and a fully client-side playground.

**Business logic:** ArchLang is free and open-source, MIT, published on npm as
`@chanmeng666/archlang`. Its strategic role is twofold. As an artifact
it is a credential — an original programming language with a
hand-written compiler, a real diagnostic system, domain-specific
static analysis, and full IDE tooling, built and versioned with
engineering discipline (13 feature branches, 10 ADRs). As
infrastructure it is the **engine that makes a commercial product
possible**: ArchCanvas (the paid AI design agent) depends on it,
writing parametric ArchLang rather than freehand SVG so that its
select-to-act editing, parametric sliders, and semantic diffs all
work — capabilities that only exist because the underlying plan is a
deterministic program. ArchLang deliberately ships **no MCP server**:
a CLI costs zero context until it is called, whereas an MCP schema
would sit in an agent's context window permanently, so agent access is
via the token-cheap CLI instead.

**Technical approach:** A pure-TypeScript, zero-runtime-dependency compiler: a hand-written
lexer (~388 LOC, line/col tracking), a recursive-descent parser
(~860 LOC), and a separate expression evaluator (~614 LOC), all
isomorphic so the same code runs in Node and in the browser — the
playground sends nothing to a server. The heavy or native pieces
(resvg for PNG raster, clipper2-wasm for polygon geometry, pdfkit for
PDF) are **optional and lazily imported** behind `webpackIgnore` /
`@vite-ignore` guards, so a browser bundle never tries to resolve them
and a default install pulls none of them. Output determinism is
enforced with property tests (fast-check) and visual-regression tests
(pixelmatch). The diagnostic layer — 74 stable `E_*`/`W_*` codes, each
with a byte span and a suggested fix, surfaced as data and even as a
self-describing error-SVG card — is the deliberate foundation of the
agent story: an LLM can author a plan, read the structured errors, and
self-correct in a tight loop without a human or an image in the middle.

**Outcomes:** A complete, published language: 192 solo commits over ~2.5 weeks of
intensive development (2026-06-25 → 2026-07-10), 80 source files
(~19.7k LOC) against 82 test files, released to npm at v1.12.1 across
13 versioned feature branches with 10 architecture decision records.
The surface area is unusually complete for a solo language project —
8 primitives, 4 export formats, 8 lint-rule modules, a circulation
analyzer, a repair pass, a ~20-command CLI, a full LSP, a VS Code
extension, a client-side playground, a VitePress docs site, and a
GitHub Action that renders every ```arch fence in a repo's Markdown.
Most tellingly, it is already load-bearing: ArchCanvas's first
in-browser use of the core drove real hardening (the v1.12.1
client-side-import fix, repair-pass robustness), proving the engine
under a downstream product rather than in isolation.

**My role:** Sole author of everything — the language design, the hand-written
lexer / parser / evaluator, the diagnostic system, all four export
backends, the lint and circulation and repair pipelines, the LSP and
VS Code extension, the CodeMirror playground, the docs site, and the
npm packaging. 192 of 192 commits are mine.
### 4. ArchCanvas — 'Your AI architect — dimensioned floor plans you can actually build from.' An AI design agent that turns a natural-language brief into ONE strong floor plan — written as parametric ArchLang (Chan's own DSL), compiled to real dimensioned SVG, plus a GPT-Image rendering grounded on that exact plan — then refined conversationally on an infinite zoomable canvas where edits morph in place. Select-to-act editing, a review mode that overlays circulation and pins critique, parametric sliders, and export of the whole design history as a real git repo. Refinement mutates the plan in place — it never regenerates it.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-14]
- **URL:** https://archcanvas.chanmeng.org/
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.3 (App Router) + React 19 + TypeScript 5.8, Vercel AI SDK (ai 4.3, @ai-sdk/openai) + OpenAI 6.45 — orchestrator migrated off GPT-4o to a newer reasoning model for tool use; GPT-4o retained for vision, GPT Image grounded renderings (view-aware model chain: aerial vs eye-level), @chanmeng666/archlang 1.12.1 (Chan's own declarative floor-plan compiler, consumed as a package), Neon Postgres + Drizzle ORM (9 tables, schema-push), Stripe 17 (credits ledger, dual live/test mode), Cloudinary (rendering storage), react-zoom-pan-pinch (infinite zoomable canvas), isomorphic-git + memfs + fflate (export design history as a real git repo, in-memory), jose (JWT/JWKS auth), Zod validation · shadcn/ui + Tailwind 4 + Framer Motion + GSAP, Docker canary-then-swap deploy over SSH (DigitalOcean VPS, Traefik + Let's Encrypt, Cloudflare Full-Strict)
- **Commits (solo):** 269 (2026-04-08 → 2026-07-10, ~3 months); sole author
- **Surfaces:** 13 pages · 28 API route handlers · 71 components · 190 source files
- **Database:** 9 Drizzle tables on Neon (projects · designFrames · agentThreads · credits · creditTransactions · shares · generationAttempts · …)
- **Agent tools:** 7 (askDesignQuestions · generateFloorPlanDSL[new plan] · editFloorPlanDSL[minimal diff] · generateRendering · critiquePlan · analyzeReference[vision] · analyzeFloorPlan)
- **Generation unit:** one generation = plan + grounded rendering + critique, produced as a single atomic set; edits, parametric tweaks, re-renders, and review mode all operate on that set without re-generating it
- **Git export:** in-memory isomorphic-git repo (memfs), one commit per design version — the commit log replays the design conversation (cap 200 commits / 25 MB)
- **Deploy:** private repo → GitHub Actions → GHCR → canary-then-swap over SSH (boot canary, poll /api/health, swap only if healthy) on a DO VPS behind Traefik + Cloudflare
**Summary:** ArchCanvas is an AI design agent for architecture and self-builders. A
user describes a build in natural language; the agent asks a few
clarifying questions, then designs one strong floor plan — written as
parametric ArchLang and compiled to a real dimensioned SVG (with room
labels and measurements), plus a photorealistic GPT-Image rendering
grounded on a raster of that exact plan. From there it is conversational:
on an infinite zoomable canvas, edits morph the plan in place, a version
timeline scrubs the lineage, a review mode overlays walking-circulation
and pins anchored critique with one-click "have AI fix," and a parametric
panel exposes the plan's own variables as live sliders. The whole design
history exports as a real git repository. Refinement never regenerates:
edits, parametric tweaks, re-renders, and review all mutate the existing
plan in place rather than asking the model for a fresh one.

**Impact:** An AI design agent for architects that productizes Chan's own ArchLang DSL — it writes parametric ArchLang (not freehand SVG) compiled to real dimensioned plans, with grounded GPT-Image renderings, a zoomable canvas, a review mode, and git-repo export. Solo-built, 269 commits.

**Problem:** AI floor-plan tools produce pretty pictures you cannot build from and
cannot precisely edit. An image-generated plan has no real dimensions,
no guarantee a wall is straight, and no handle to grab when you want
the kitchen two metres wider — you re-prompt and hope. Meanwhile
professional CAD is powerful but slow and expert-only, and neither
world lets you simply *talk* to your design. ArchCanvas exists to close
that gap: a plan you can converse your way to, that is nonetheless a
real, dimensioned, buildable artifact you can keep editing precisely.

**Audience:** Architects and designers who want to move fast from brief to a
credible dimensioned plan, and self-builders / renovators who have a
vision but not CAD skills and need something they (or a builder) can
actually work from. Because every plan is real ArchLang underneath, the
same product serves a careful professional who wants to tweak a wall
thickness by a slider and a first-timer who just wants to describe
their dream house and see it — and refine it — in plain language.

**Product logic:** The studio runs in two acts. **Act I** is a full-screen briefing
conversation: the user picks a typology (residential, accessible
clinic, open-plan office, passive house) and describes the build; the
agent asks two-to-five clarifying questions inline, then generates ONE
plan — deliberately not a grid of options — as parametric ArchLang,
compiles it to a dimensioned SVG with `data-arch-id` anchors, renders a
grounded GPT-Image visualization, and critiques it. That single
generation turn is the only point at which the agent is asked for a
brand-new plan.

**Act II** is a canvas-first studio, and nothing in it regenerates.
Edits are conversational and **morph the plan in place** — the parent
geometry fades to a dashed ghost, a semantic-diff chip shows what
changed, and a version-lineage timeline lets you scrub the history.
**Select-to-act**: click a room, door, or window and a floating action
bar offers verb chips (Enlarge / Shrink / Widen / Move) plus
agent-suggested scoped edits. A **Parametric Tweaks** panel turns the
plan's own ArchLang `let` variables (room sizes, counts, wall
thickness) into live sliders that recompile instantly with no LLM call
at all. **Review mode** toggles ArchLang's circulation overlay
(walk routes, pinch points) and pins anchored critique findings, each
with a one-click "have AI fix" — display-only, never auto-applied. The
finished design exports as SVG/PNG/DXF/PDF, or as a real git repository
whose commit log is the design conversation.

**Business logic:** The unit of work is one generation turn — plan + grounded rendering +
critique, produced as a single atomic set — and everything downstream
of it is refinement rather than regeneration. That split is deliberate:
a design task's value is lumpy (you want a few strong plans, then you
iterate on them for a long time), so every edit, parametric tweak,
re-render, and review action mutates the existing frame in place, and
the model is re-invoked only when the user genuinely asks for new work.
The moat is the ArchLang pairing: because plans are a deterministic
program rather than pixels, ArchCanvas can offer editing, tweaking,
diffing, and git export that an image-generation competitor structurally
cannot.

**Technical approach:** A single Next.js 15 monolith (React 19, TypeScript 5.8) over a 9-table
Drizzle schema on Neon Postgres, with Stripe credits, Cloudinary
rendering storage, and jose-based auth. The AI layer is an orchestrated
tool-agent on the Vercel AI SDK: the orchestrator was deliberately
migrated off GPT-4o (which under-used tools and wrote non-parametric
DSL) to a newer reasoning model, while GPT-4o is retained for vision
(reference-image analysis). Its seven tools center on
`generateFloorPlanDSL` — write parametric ArchLang, compile it through
Chan's own `@chanmeng666/archlang` package, pass a ship-gate, save a
frame — with `editFloorPlanDSL` as the minimal-diff follow-up.
Renderings are a **grounded, view-aware chain**: the compiled plan is
rasterized and sent as the grounding image to GPT Image, with a
model order chosen per view (aerial vs eye-level) so the render matches
the real geometry instead of hallucinating a layout. `designFrames`
stores each version's ArchLang source, compiled SVG, rendering URLs,
lint warnings, review findings, and a self-referential `parentFrameId`
for lineage — which is exactly what the in-memory git export
(isomorphic-git + memfs, zipped with fflate) walks root-to-tip to build
a real repository. Deployment is a canary-then-swap over SSH: build a
Docker image, push to GHCR, boot a health-checked canary, and only swap
traffic if `/api/health` passes — otherwise abort untouched.

**Outcomes:** Live at archcanvas.chanmeng.org, solo-built across 269 commits over
roughly three months (2026-04-08 → 2026-07-10): 13 pages, 28 API route
handlers, 71 components, and 190 source files over a 9-table schema.
The product is unusually rigorous about testing its own thesis — a
`generationAttempts` telemetry table tracks first-pass ArchLang
validity per model, and a `bench` harness runs briefs through both the
DSL pipeline and a legacy freehand-pixel pipeline as a control group,
so the "DSL beats pixels" claim is measured, not asserted. It is also
the proving ground that hardened ArchLang: ArchCanvas's real
in-browser use of the compiler drove concrete upstream fixes.

**My role:** Sole author of the entire product — the two-act studio and canvas UX,
the AI orchestrator and its seven tools, the grounded view-aware
rendering chain, the ArchLang integration, the 9-table schema and
credit/billing system, the git-repo export, the falsifiability
instruments, and the canary-then-swap deployment. And of ArchLang
itself, the DSL it is built on. 269 of 269 commits are mine.
### 5. She Sharp — Solo-built production platform that digitized, automated, and AI-upgraded NZ's
leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust CC57025, 2,200+ members,
8,000+ women supported) — replacing Webflow + spreadsheets with a real member
engine. GPT-4o-mini AI mentor matching (5-weight scoring + admin review),
three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor / mentee / admin), Stripe billing,
and a custom Slack ecosystem (event-collector bot, weekly mentorship-stats
digest, NZ government funding crawler, Claude `sync-event-from-slack` skill)
that turned hours of volunteer manual work into one-command automation.
648/748 commits (86.6%) solo across 10 months.

[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://www.shesharp.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/NZ-SheSharp/she-sharp
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.6 canary (App Router, Turbopack dev), React 19.1.2, TypeScript 5.8, Drizzle ORM 0.43, Neon Postgres (serverless pool), NextAuth 5 beta (Google + GitHub OAuth + bcrypt credentials), @auth/drizzle-adapter, OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (temp 0.3, max_tokens 1000, JSON-object response_format), Upstash Redis (AI match cache + queue), Stripe 20 (Customer Portal for self-service membership management), @slack/web-api (event-collector + interactive handlers), @octokit/rest (Slack-skill auto-commits), Claude agent skill: sync-event-from-slack, Resend (transactional email), Cloudinary (image hosting + transformations), Tailwind CSS 4.1, shadcn/ui + Radix UI (20+ primitives), @dnd-kit (kanban-style admin workflows), Recharts 3 + @number-flow/react (dashboard analytics), Framer Motion + Lenis (landing-page polish), Three.js + OGL (hero shader effects), Zod 3 (validation everywhere — Slack-bot LLM output gating, form schemas), React Hook Form + @hookform/resolvers, Vercel Edge Network + Vercel Cron
- **Total commits:** 748
- **Solo commits:** 648 (86.6%) — Lesley Gao authored ~100 frontend commits (13.4%)
- **Commits since 2026-01-01:** 345 (intensive H1 build-out)
- **Build window:** 2025-07-22 → 2026-05-11 (~10 months active)
- **API routes:** 95 (app/api/**/route.ts)
- **Pages:** 53 (app/**/page.tsx)
- **Components:** 145 (components/**/*.tsx)
- **Database schema:** 1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, 34 pgTables
- **SQL migrations:** 27 (0000_soft_the_anarchist → 0026_brief_invisible_woman)
- **Lib modules:** matching, slack-bot, stripe, funding, programmes, recruitment, invitations, mentorship, email, auth, db (11 namespaced)
- **Cron jobs:** 3 (process-queue, weekly-funding-crawl, weekly-mentorship-stats)
- **Funding crawler sources:** 6 NZ government feeds (beehive RSS, data.govt CKAN, GETS RSS, MBIE HTML, Treasury RSS, women.govt HTML)
- **AI matching weights:** MBTI 15% + Skill 35% + Goal 30% + Industry 10% + Logistics 10%
- **AI matching score range:** 0-100 overall + 5 per-dimension subscores + confidence (high/medium/low)
- **AI retry policy:** 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s × 2^n), batch concurrency 5, 100ms inter-batch delay
- **Members:** 2,200+ registered / 1,000+ active
- **Women supported:** 8,000+ lifetime
- **Corporate partners:** 50+
- **Events since 2014:** 84+
- **Industry mentors:** 18
- **Mentorship success rate:** 85% (5-6× higher promotion likelihood for mentees per She Sharp's outcomes data)
- **Migration crawler assets:** 1,748 images / 707 MB / 11 JSON files / 85 events + 50 Humanitix tickets + 15 podcasts + 121 photo galleries + 7 press articles + 118 pages
- **Part of:** She Sharp (https://shesharp.org.nz)
- **Related projects:** HER WAKA — Employment Programme for Women in Auckland, Aotearoa AI Hackathon 2026 — Event Q&A AI Assistant (She Sharp), Touch the World, Talk to Code
**Summary:** Solo-built production member platform for She Sharp — New Zealand's
leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust, 2,200+ registered
members / 8,000+ women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate partners, 84+
events since 2014). Replaces the previous Webflow site. 648 of 748
commits (86.6%) authored solo. Headline features: GPT-4o-mini-powered
AI mentor-mentee matching with five-dimensional scoring + human review,
role-aware mentor / mentee / admin dashboards with dual-role support,
custom Slack ecosystem (event-collector bot, volunteer webhooks, weekly
stats digest, NZ funding-opportunities crawler), a Claude agent skill
`sync-event-from-slack` that automates event page publication, Stripe
tier billing, and full admin + audit systems. Plus three supporting
projects: a Python crawler that migrated 1,748 images / 707 MB / 85
events from the old Webflow site, the Her Waka MSD-funded teaching
site (Mintlify, curriculum authored solo), and a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz with a 10-point recommendations report.

**Impact:** Digitized and AI-upgraded NZ's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (2,200+ members, 8,000+ supported) — 86.6% solo-built platform with AI mentor matching, three role dashboards, and a Slack automation stack that replaced hours of volunteer manual work.

**Problem:** She Sharp is New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit — a
registered charitable trust founded by Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley),
AI/NLP researcher and Director of Women in Tech at AUT, after she
experienced first-hand the gap of being part of the 20% minority of
women in technology. The community has grown to 2,200+ registered
members, 8,000+ women supported across its history, 50+ corporate
partners, and 84+ events since 2014. The original website was a
Webflow build, and Webflow had become the bottleneck: the platform
capabilities the mission required — membership tiers, mentor / mentee
/ admin role permissions, AI-driven mentor matching, Stripe billing,
Slack-integrated operations, automated event management, recruitment
pipelines, audit logging — are precisely what no SaaS site builder
can express. The leadership team was running a 2,200-member community
through spreadsheets, Slack threads, and manual outreach. She Sharp
needed a real platform.

**Audience:** Active members of the She Sharp community: women in technology at
every career stage, from undergraduate students considering STEM
majors through senior engineers serving as mentors. The platform
supports three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor, mentee, admin)
plus dual-role compatibility — a mid-career engineer can
simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored by a senior, without
special-cased data modelling. Additional audiences served on adjacent
surfaces: Her Waka programme participants (women re-entering the
workforce, served by the dedicated MSD-funded teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz), volunteer and ambassador candidates
(screened through the AI recruitment pipeline), the 50+ corporate
partners (sponsor packages tied to event visibility and member
access), and She Sharp's operational team (Slack-integrated
workflows). 1,000+ members are actively engaged today, drawn from a
registered base of 2,200+ and a lifetime supported population of
8,000+ women.

**Product logic:** The new platform replaces the old Webflow site with a real member
system anchored on three coexisting dashboards. A user signs up via
email/password (bcrypt) or OAuth (Google + GitHub), then independently
activates any combination of mentor, mentee, or admin roles — duality
is the **schema-level design principle**, not a special case. The
`user_roles` table has one row per (user, role_type) tuple with its
own `is_active`, `verified_at`, and `activation_step` columns, so a
mid-career engineer can simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored
by a senior with zero special-cased data modelling. The dashboard
layer (`(dashboard)/dashboard/{mentor-profile,mentee-profile,
mentorship,meetings,admin,account}`) reads the active-roles set and
composes role-specific cards into a single unified view.

**The headline feature is the AI mentor-mentee matching engine**,
implemented as a two-stage pipeline in `lib/matching/`. Stage one is
a rule-based pre-filter that rapidly scores candidate pairs on basic
compatibility (skill set intersection, industry overlap, location/
meeting-format compatibility). Stage two passes promising pairs to
GPT-4o-mini at `temperature: 0.3`, `max_tokens: 1000`, with
`response_format: { type: 'json_object' }`, against an explicit
scoring rubric defined in `lib/matching/prompts.ts`:

  - **MBTI Compatibility — 15%** (cognitive function complementarity,
    communication style)
  - **Skill Alignment — 35%** (mentor's expert skills vs mentee's
    learning goals, expertise depth vs learning breadth)
  - **Goal Alignment — 30%** (career trajectory compatibility, mentor's
    ability to guide toward stated goals, shared values)
  - **Industry Match — 10%** (direct + adjacent + transferable industry
    knowledge)
  - **Logistics — 10%** (meeting format, location proximity, time
    availability)

Each call returns a 0-100 overall + per-dimension subscores +
natural-language explanation + recommendation + confidence (high /
medium / low) + matchingFactors (strengths / challenges /
growthOpportunities), Zod-validated against `AIMatchResponseSchema`
before persistence. **Three production-hardness layers wrap the
OpenAI call**: (1) **Upstash Redis cache** keyed by mentor:mentee
pair so repeat scoring is free; (2) **exponential-backoff retries**
(3 attempts, 1000ms × 2^attempt, with rate-limit/timeout
classification); (3) a **deterministic rule-based fallback** with
the same weighted formula that activates when OpenAI is unavailable,
explicitly marking `confidenceLevel: 'low'` and
`recommendation: 'manual review'` so admins know to look closer.
Batch processing caps at 5 concurrent calls with 100ms inter-batch
delay to respect API limits.

Matches above threshold surface to admin (`/api/admin/matching`)
for human approval before pairing — conservative human-in-the-loop
automation, not speculative auto-pairing. A **mentee matching queue**
(`lib/matching/queue-service.ts` + Vercel Cron at
`/api/cron/process-queue`) holds unmatched mentees in priority order
when mentor capacity is full and re-evaluates as slots open.
Meetings are typed (intro / regular / milestone / final), tracked
with action items (JSONB), notes, feedback, and 1-5 ratings.

**The Stripe-integrated membership flow has a novel activation
architecture**: it doesn't instantly grant access. The architecture
is **form → checkout → invitation code → activation**. A prospective
mentee fills the public mentee form (`/api/forms/mentee/public`),
which creates a `mentee_form_submissions` row. They get redirected
to Stripe Checkout. On `checkout.session.completed` webhook, the
system creates a `membership_purchases` record AND generates a
unique `invitation_code` (`createPaymentInvitationCode`) which is
emailed via Resend. The user must redeem the code to activate their
account, guaranteeing email ↔ payment ↔ account linkage and
preventing orphaned accounts. Self-service via Stripe Customer
Portal. Webhook signature verification on every event. Membership
lifecycle transitions update `user_memberships` + `activity_logs`
atomically.

The platform also runs She Sharp's events infrastructure (workshops,
training, networking, social, THRIVE programmes — online, in-person,
or hybrid, with capacity, waitlist, and QR check-in via `/api/qr`),
three membership tiers, a tiered resource library
with download access control + access logs, transactional email via
Resend, AI-screened volunteer recruitment (`lib/recruitment/
ai-screening.ts` against `/api/admin/recruitment/[id]/ai-screen`),
sponsor inquiries with inline forms, and comprehensive audit logging
(JSONB diffs via `deep-diff` on every admin action).

**A custom Slack ecosystem is deeply integrated** through three
coordinated surfaces. (1) **Read-only event-collector bot** with
scoped tokens (`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read`) — no destructive scopes by design. (2) **Three
Vercel Cron jobs**: `weekly-mentorship-stats` posts engagement
digest to `#mentorships` (recently hardened to survive cold Neon
connection pools), `weekly-funding-crawl` runs the NZ government
funding crawler over **6 official sources** (Beehive RSS, data.govt
CKAN, GETS RSS, MBIE HTML, Treasury RSS, women.govt HTML) with
dedup + relevance scoring + keyword filtering, posting a curated
Slack digest, and `process-queue` re-evaluates the matching queue.
(3) **Interactive Slack event handlers** at `/api/slack/events` and
`/api/slack/events/interactive` for in-Slack workflows.

**The most distinctive Slack-side piece** is a Claude agent skill
named `sync-event-from-slack` living at
`.claude/skills/sync-event-from-slack/`. It automates the previously-
manual work of turning a Slack event channel into a published event
page: it reads channel messages, thread replies, pinned items,
bookmarks, speaker `.docx` bios (via `mammoth`), cover posters, and
speaker headshots; downloads images and renames them per convention;
composes a structured `EventV3Schema` JSON entry against a strict
Zod schema; runs `verify-image-paths` as a CI gate; and on approval
commits to git or opens a PR. **The skill defaults to dry-run** —
no file writes, no git, no network side-effects beyond reading
Slack — unless the user explicitly says "go ahead and apply". Event
publication has gone from hours of manual editing to a single
command, with the AI bot's structured output Zod-validated as the
last line of defense before any git write.

**Business logic:** She Sharp is a registered New Zealand charitable trust. Three
operational channels now run through the platform at scale: three
membership tiers (free / basic / premium) with Stripe-integrated
flows; 50+ corporate-partner relationships (sponsor packages tied
to event visibility and member-base access); and grant-funded
programmes — notably Her Waka, an NZ Ministry of Social Development-
funded four-session employment-readiness programme for women
re-entering the workforce, run at academyEX Auckland (March–June
2026), with its own dedicated teaching site I built and authored.

The platform is the operational substrate that lets each channel
scale without proportional staff load: dashboards, matching,
membership lifecycle, communications, recruitment, and event
management are automated. The organization's mission scaling (from
the 1,500 active members publicly cited a year ago, toward the
2,200+ registered base today and beyond) depends directly on this
technical capability.

**Technical approach:** Next.js 15.6 canary (App Router, Turbopack dev) + React 19.1.2 +
TypeScript 5.8 over Drizzle ORM 0.43 on Neon Postgres (serverless
connection pool), authenticated via NextAuth 5 beta with bcrypt
credentials and Google + GitHub OAuth (via `@auth/drizzle-adapter`),
deployed on Vercel's edge network with documented sub-second TTFB.
Tailwind CSS 4.1 + shadcn/ui + Radix UI (20+ primitives) for the UI;
Framer Motion + Lenis + Three.js/OGL for landing-page polish;
@dnd-kit for admin kanban workflows; Recharts 3 + @number-flow/react
for dashboard analytics.

**The schema runs to 1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, and 34 pgTables** —
a deliberate type-safety investment. Membership tiers (free / basic /
premium), mentorship-relationship states (pending / active / paused /
completed / rejected), meeting types (intro / regular / milestone /
final), event types (workshop / networking / training / social /
thrive), location types (online / in_person / hybrid), career stages
(undergraduate → postgraduate → early_career → mid_career → senior →
career_transition), MBTI 16 personality types, recruitment stages,
invitation-code redemption states, activity-log action types — every
business concept is a typed enum rather than a string column, so
invalid states are unrepresentable at the database level. **27 SQL
migrations** (0000_soft_the_anarchist → 0026_brief_invisible_woman)
applied via a custom safe-migration wrapper with pre-deployment
snapshots and rollback SQL generation.

**95 API routes** across `app/api/**/route.ts` covering auth (11
routes including OAuth callbacks, password reset, email
verification), admin (21 routes for matching/mentee-mentor reviews/
recruitment/programmes/users/permissions/analytics/zombie-cleanup),
mentorship lifecycle (apply / approve / relationships / meetings),
events (browse / register / my-registrations / QR check-in), forms
(mentee / mentor / volunteer with public-vs-authenticated variants),
Stripe (checkout / donate / webhook), Slack (events / interactive),
cron (process-queue / weekly-funding-crawl / weekly-mentorship-stats),
resources (download with access control), notifications, file
uploads (CV + profile photo via Cloudinary). **53 pages, 145
components.**

**The AI matching engine** combines a deterministic rule-based
pre-filter with GPT-4o-mini for high-potential pairs only. The
`lib/matching/` namespace has clear separation: `prompts.ts` holds
the system rubric and per-pair prompt builder, `openai-service.ts`
owns the LLM call with retry/cache/fallback, `cache.ts` interfaces
Upstash Redis, `queue-service.ts` manages the FIFO/priority queue
for over-capacity scenarios, `service.ts` orchestrates the full
match flow, and `types.ts` defines `AIMatchResponseSchema` (Zod)
that every LLM output must satisfy. Cost stays predictable because
Stage 1 filters out incompatible pairs before they reach the LLM;
outputs stay explainable because the rubric is explicit;
availability stays high because the rule-based fallback uses the
same weighted formula. Admin override is the final gate.

**Slack integration** uses **scoped read-only tokens** by design
(`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read` — never `chat:write` from the collector). The
`sync-event-from-slack` agent skill composes existing CLI tools
(Slack fetch → image download with rename → docx parse via
`mammoth` → JSON validate against `EventV3Schema` Zod → git commit
via `@octokit/rest` or local) into a single intent-driven workflow.
**Strict Zod validation is the last line of defense before any
git write** — an invalid LLM patch is rejected with a Slack-
friendly error, never persisted. The `weekly-funding-crawl` cron
runs `lib/funding/{fetcher,dedup,scoring,keywords}.ts` over 6
government RSS/HTML sources, scores by relevance keywords, dedups
across sources, and posts a curated Slack digest. The recent
`weekly-mentorship-stats` hardening (`84f788f`) is a small but
revealing detail: Neon's serverless pool can return stale
connections to a cold Vercel Cron worker, so the cron now retries
once on the specific `ECONNRESET`/`Connection terminated` error
class before failing the run.

**Image storage on Cloudinary** with on-the-fly transformations;
**transactional email via Resend** with role-specific templates
(mentorship-emails.ts + recruitment-emails.ts + membership
confirmation); **payments via Stripe** with Customer Portal for
self-service membership management; **audit logging** uses
`deep-diff` to capture JSONB before/after on every admin mutation.

The role spans beyond the main platform. Three supporting projects
round out the contribution. **she-sharp-crawl** — a Python crawler
(BeautifulSoup4, Requests) that migrated content from the old
Webflow site: 1,748 images totaling 707 MB across 11 modular JSON
files, covering 85 SheSharp events, 50 Humanitix ticketed events
(with banner images upgraded 500 px → 1,600 px), 15 podcasts, 121
photo galleries, 7 news / press articles, and 118 site pages. Image
deduplication and Wayback Machine fallback for dead links.
**her-waka** — the Mintlify-powered teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz for the MSD-funded Her Waka programme; I
authored both the platform and the curriculum (four workshops
covering AI & Future of Work, #IAmRemarkable + Vibe Coding,
Cybersecurity, Personal Branding, plus 8+ self-paced tutorials at
three difficulty tiers). **serpdata-api-test** — a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz built on Node 22 native fetch over the
SerpData API, running ~20 live Google queries across eight analysis
sections (brand visibility, topical keyword rankings, NZ competitor
matrix, mobile-vs-desktop gap, cross-market reach, SERP feature
inventory) plus a 10-point prioritized recommendations report
(e.g., "SheSharp ranks #4 desktop / not in mobile top 10 for 'women
in tech New Zealand' — high-priority technical SEO gap").

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed and operating She Sharp's full member lifecycle
for ~10 months (first commit 2025-07-22, latest 2026-05-11). Commit
volume: **748 total commits, 648 (86.6%) authored solo by Chan
Meng**, with the remaining ~100 commits by Lesley Gao on frontend
design and code. **345 of those commits landed in 2026 H1 alone** —
the platform is in active build-out, not in maintenance mode. The
platform now runs She Sharp's membership, mentorship, events,
sponsor relationships, volunteer recruitment pipeline, and internal
team operations across **95 API routes, 53 pages, 145 components,
27 SQL migrations, and an 11-namespace `lib/` library** (matching,
slack-bot, stripe, funding, programmes, recruitment, invitations,
mentorship, email, auth, db) — the technical infrastructure for an
organization with 2,200+ registered members, 1,000+ active, 8,000+
women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate partners, 84+ events since
2014, 18 industry mentors, and a reported 85% mentorship success
rate (mentees achieve 5-6× higher promotion likelihood per She
Sharp's own outcomes data).

**The Her Waka programme launched in March 2026** with NZ Ministry
of Social Development funding, using the dedicated teaching site I
built (herwaka.shesharp.org.nz) and the curriculum I authored —
