4 workshops + 8 tutorials in 3 tiers. **The SEO audit** identified
actionable competitive gaps — notably the desktop/mobile ranking
disparity on the core query "women in tech New Zealand" — and
informed near-term content strategy. **The migration crawler** enabled
a clean cutover from Webflow without losing institutional knowledge.
**The Slack-bot + agent-skill pairing** replaced an hours-long
manual event-publication workflow with a single dry-run-default
command; the most-recent cron-hardening commit (`84f788f`, "make
weekly-mentorship-stats survive cold Neon pools") is the kind of
small production-resilience detail that only emerges from operating
the system, not just building it.

**My role:** Senior Full Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead. Recruited directly
by founder Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley) — AI/NLP researcher and
Director of Women in Tech at AUT — specifically to deliver the AI
and full-stack senior engineering that Webflow couldn't, and that
the She Sharp team didn't have in-house.

Solo full-stack ownership of the main platform: product architecture,
database schema (1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, 34 pgTables, 27 SQL
migrations), AI matching pipeline (5-weight rubric + Upstash Redis
cache + rule-based fallback + admin human-in-the-loop), Stripe
billing with the form→checkout→invitation-code activation flow,
Slack ecosystem (read-only event-collector, 3 weekly Cron digests,
interactive event handlers), the `sync-event-from-slack` Claude
agent skill with strict Zod validation and dry-run default, admin
systems, deployments, observability, and operational support.
Lesley Gao contributed some frontend design and frontend code
(~100 commits, 13.4% of total); the remaining 648 commits (86.6%)
are mine. Beyond the main platform I also designed and built the
data migration crawler that bootstrapped the launch
(she-sharp-crawl), the Her Waka teaching site (her-waka) and its
complete four-workshop + 8-tutorial curriculum, and the
professional SEO audit and recommendations report
(serpdata-api-test) on shesharp.org.nz — operating across
engineering, instructional design, and strategic analysis under
one role.
### 6. Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz) — Agent-ready AI career agent (vitex.org.nz) — turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30s via an 8-step pipeline. The whole product is reachable over an authenticated public HTTP API, plus a vitex-cli and a stdio MCP server (Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor); a DB partial-unique index makes each generation idempotent, so an SSE reconnect or a concurrent retry never re-runs the pipeline. Vercel AI SDK + GPT-4o + Typst local compile (<100ms), on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-14]
- **URL:** https://www.vitex.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/easy-resume
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript 5, Vercel AI SDK v6, @ai-sdk/openai, OpenAI GPT-4o, Zod schemas, Typst (local binary), Neon Postgres, Drizzle ORM, Upstash Redis, Stack Auth, Stripe, Cloudinary, shadcn/ui + Neobrutalism, Framer Motion, Public v1 HTTP API (OpenAPI spec + curl playbook in skill.md), vitex-cli (thin API client, bin `vitex`), MCP server (`vitex mcp` via npx, stdio — Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor), Docker, Traefik, DigitalOcean VPS, GitHub Actions CI/CD
- **Commits (solo):** 372 (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-10, ~18 months); essentially sole author
- **Surfaces:** 11 pages · 29 API route handlers · 25 components · 222 source files (~30k LOC)
- **Pipeline:** 8 steps (parse JD ∥ parse background → match → tailor + faithfulness gate → ATS score ∥ cover letter → render → Typst compile)
- **Templates:** 7 AI-auto-selected (academic · banking · compact · creative · executive · modern-cv · two-column)
- **Agent surfaces:** one core, N thin adapters — web SSE + public REST v1 (API key) + hosted OAuth-protected MCP + published vitex-cli with a stdio MCP; 9 MCP tools; full OAuth 2.1 AS with dynamic client registration + PKCE
- **Idempotency:** a generation is committed exactly once, and only after a PDF actually compiles; a DB partial-unique index survives SSE reconnects + concurrent races, so retries and refinements never duplicate a run
- **Models:** two-tier routing (cheap extract tier + reasoning tier, env-overridable); deterministic no-LLM ATS scoring + anti-hallucination faithfulness gate
- **PDF engine:** local Typst binary compile <100ms, SHA-256-cached; no external document API
- **Tests:** 215 Vitest test files (high density)
**Summary:** AI Career Agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. A user pastes a job
description, describes their background in free text, and an
**8-step AI pipeline** (JD parsing → background parsing → match
analysis → resume tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter →
document generation → server-side PDF compilation) streams the
result into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + matching
cover letter in ~30 seconds. Typst compiles PDFs locally in
under 100 ms across 7 AI-auto-selected professional templates —
no external document-generation API. The product is
**agent-ready**: every capability is reachable over an
authenticated **public v1 HTTP API** (the web UI and API share
one pipeline core), and a thin **vitex-cli** plus a stdio **MCP
server** (`vitex mcp`) let terminals and coding agents (Claude
Desktop/Code, Cursor) drive the whole product without a browser.
The pipeline is **idempotent**: a generation is committed exactly
once, and only after a PDF actually compiles — a database
partial-unique index holds that invariant through SSE reconnects
and concurrent retries. Natural-language refinement then applies
targeted edits to the existing document rather than re-running the
whole pipeline. Production runs as a Docker container on a
**DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + TLS),
shipped continuously by **GitHub Actions**.

**Impact:** Agent-ready AI career agent — an 8-step pipeline turns a JD + free-text background into a tailored ATS-optimised resume + cover letter in ~30s, usable over a public HTTP API, a CLI, and an MCP server; idempotent generation (committed only on compile), Typst PDF compile <100ms.

**Problem:** Most AI resume tools are one of two failure modes: (a) a
thin GPT wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a
resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template
intelligence; or (b) a SaaS that lets you fill out a form
and burns 90 seconds spinning a hosted Chromium to render
the same template every other user gets. Both miss the
actual job: the candidate needs the resume *tailored to the
specific JD they're applying to*, the document needs to
survive ATS parsing, the visual template needs to match the
target industry/role, and the round-trip needs to feel
instant. None of those are true of "paste your CV into
ChatGPT."

**Audience:** Three groups. **Job seekers in real applications** —
especially in NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where
Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz lives
(the NZ domain is deliberate). **Career-change candidates**
who need every application tailored — the people for whom
re-writing a resume per JD is the actual bottleneck.
**AI-engineering portfolio reviewers** evaluating Chan's
ability to ship a production-grade multi-step AI agent with
real users, real billing, and real DevOps — not a demo.

**Product logic:** User flow is intentionally short: (1) **Homepage** — paste
a job description + describe your professional background
in free text. No CV upload, no form filling, no field-by-field
data entry. (2) **Generate** — click run; the 8-step AI
pipeline streams progress via SSE, the user watches each
stage complete (parsing JD → parsing background → matching
→ tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → Typst compile),
and arrives at the **Editor** view with the PDF preview,
ATS score with actionable feedback, the matching cover
letter, and a **natural-language refinement box**
("make the leadership section stronger" / "shorten the
intro paragraph" — the AI regenerates that slice).
(3) **Refine & export** — download the PDF or generate a
public share link with a unique token. Persistent **resume
management dashboard** for active users.

**Business logic:** Generation capacity is gated per event rather than left
unbounded: each run is a 7-step OpenAI pipeline plus a Typst
compile, so the ledger commits a run exactly once — and only
after a PDF actually compiles — which keeps capacity
predictable and makes a failed or retried run a non-event.
Public **share links via unique tokens** give the product
organic distribution: a candidate who lands a job credits
Vitex on LinkedIn, the share link becomes social proof, the
referenced resume itself is a working landing page
demonstrating the product. The whole stack — Auth + DB +
Cache + Payments + AI + PDF rendering — is production-shaped
for a real user base, not a demo.

**Technical approach:** **AI agent layer.** The pipeline is **Vercel AI SDK v6**
with the OpenAI provider (`@ai-sdk/openai`) across **eight
sequential stages** on two-tier model routing (a cheap extract
tier for parsing, a reasoning tier for tailoring; env-overridable),
each producing a **Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD Parsing** — extract role, seniority, must-have
   skills, nice-to-haves, company signals
2. **Background Parsing** — convert the user's free-text
   description into a structured skill / experience model
3. **Match Analysis** — compute the candidate↔JD fit profile
4. **Resume Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise
   the candidate's history toward the parsed JD
5. **ATS Scoring** — assess parse-ability against ATS rules,
   emit actionable feedback
6. **Cover Letter Generation** — produce a matching cover
   letter from the same parsed pair
7. **Render** — auto-select a template + seeded design tokens,
   emit Typst source for the resume + cover letter
8. **Compile** — compile Typst → PDF bytes server-side; the PDF
   is the deliverable, so the run is committed only here

Immediately after tailoring (step 4) a deterministic
**faithfulness gate** re-checks the output for fabricated skills
or employers and triggers one bounded corrective re-tailor — an
anti-hallucination guard with no LLM in the check itself. Each
stage streams progress to the client via **Server-Sent Events**
so the user sees the agent thinking instead of a spinner. The system disables OpenAI strict JSON schema
mode where Zod uses `.optional()` (commit log captures the
diagnosis), and reads JD + background from `sessionStorage`
instead of URL params so deep links don't leak input data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDF generation runs
a **local Typst binary** in the Docker image, compiling
each document in **under 100 ms** without calling any
external API. The project originally rendered with LaTeX
and migrated to Typst on 2026-04-06 — the same day as the
Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS migration — because
Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds
and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The Docker
image pre-populates the Typst package cache for FontAwesome
so cold container starts don't pay the package-fetch cost.
**7 professional templates** (academic, banking, compact,
creative, executive, modern-cv, two-column) are AI-auto-selected
based on parsed industry / role signals; the renderer also ships
FontAwesome 6 contact icons (replacing the emoji icons of
an earlier iteration).

**Persistence + product stack.**
- **Neon serverless Postgres** with **Drizzle ORM** for
  user resumes, generation history, and credit ledgers
- **Upstash Redis** for short-lived cache + rate-control
  state
- **Stack Auth** for the authentication surface
- **Stripe** for the credits-ledger payment integration
- **Cloudinary** for user uploads and rendered-PDF storage
- **nanoid** for the shareable-token URL scheme

**UI.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The
**Neobrutalism design system** (hard shadows, thick borders,
high contrast) is the deliberate visual identity — chosen
and refined in the Phase 3 UI/UX overhaul branded
internally as *"sell results, not tools"*. shadcn/ui +
Tailwind for primitives; Framer Motion for transitions;
react-hook-form + Zod resolvers for input validation.

**Deployment — three migrations, one production.** Started
on **Railway**, migrated to **Cloudflare Workers**
(2026-02-22), then migrated again to a **DigitalOcean VPS**
(2026-04-06) because the Typst binary needs a real
filesystem and a long-lived container. Production now runs
as a single **Docker container** on the VPS behind
**Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), continuously
deployed by **GitHub Actions** on every push to the
deployment branch. The Dockerfile uses `curl` (not `wget`)
and `xz-utils` for the Typst tarball — both fixes captured
in the commit log as real edge-case failures during the
VPS migration.

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users
end-to-end from pasted JD to compiled PDF. **372 commits over
~18 months** (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-10), essentially sole-authored.
Three
deployment migrations and a LaTeX → Typst document-engine
swap shipped without service outage — every migration step
is in the commit log including the specific Docker
fixes (`xz-utils`, `curl`, FontAwesome package pre-cache),
the strict-JSON-schema regression diagnosis, and the
`sessionStorage` migration for sensitive input. Mobile
reliability + responsive UI pass shipped 2026-05-09 after
the production hardening phase.

**My role:** Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed
the 8-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages
(migrating the editor from CopilotKit to Vercel AI SDK
agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built
the Stripe credit system, configured Stack Auth, designed
the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens,
executed all three deployment migrations (Railway → CF
Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst
engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes for `xz-utils`
and the Typst FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the
Neobrutalism UI in the "sell results, not tools" Phase 3
overhaul, built the natural-language refinement loop in
the Editor, and continues to operate the production
service as recently as the May 2026 mobile reliability
pass.

## Notable Open Source Projects

### Google News MCP Server — Early-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 5 weeks after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 MCP launch) — PulseMCP "Top Pick", featured in Skywork AI deep-dive review, distributed across 15+ MCP catalogs
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-news
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, stdio transport, SerpAPI, axios, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666)

**Summary:** An early-ecosystem MCP server — first commit **Dec 30 2024, just
35 days after Anthropic's public launch of the Model Context
Protocol on Nov 25 2024**. Brings Google News search to AI
assistants like Claude Desktop, Cline, and Cursor through SerpAPI,
with automatic topical categorization (AI & Technology, Business,
Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and multi-language /
multi-region support. Carries the **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge**
with 26.1k estimated visitors and a #1,105 global popularity rank.
Featured in three independent deep-dive write-ups: Skywork AI's
*"Chan Meng's Google News MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Guide to
Real-Time News Integration"*, Skywork's *"Google News & Trends
MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Deep Dive"*, and AgentSkillsHub's
*"Install Guide & Security Review"*. Distributed across **15+
MCP catalogs and registries** — Smithery, mcp.so, Glama (A
license rating, 122 stars), PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers,
MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, mcpservers.org,
Archestra, serp.ai, mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub,
MagicSlides — and npm-published as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`. Paired with a companion early
server, `server-google-jobs` (first commit Dec 31 2024) —
together 169 solo commits across the two servers. Beyond shipping
code, I contributed early ecosystem troubleshooting via
[modelcontextprotocol/servers issue #76](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers/issues/76)
(opened Nov 27 2024) — the Windows + NVM PATH workaround
referenced in both server READMEs as primary troubleshooting
guidance.

**Impact:** Earliest-ecosystem MCP server (shipped 35 days after Anthropic's Nov 2024 MCP launch) — now listed across 15+ MCP catalogs, earned PulseMCP's 'Top Pick' badge and Glama's A-rating, used by AI assistants worldwide for current-events search.

**Problem:** Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol specification in
November 2024 as the open standard for connecting AI assistants
like Claude to external tools and data sources — but in the first
weeks after launch, the ecosystem had only a handful of reference
servers and no real way for AI assistants to search current news.
Generative engines (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity) were increasingly
being asked "what's happening with X today" — questions an LLM
cannot answer from training data alone. The gap was concrete:
Claude Desktop users wanted current-events search; the MCP server
catalog had nothing for Google News.

A second, related friction surfaced almost immediately: Windows
users running Node Version Manager couldn't get any npm-based MCP
server (filesystem, github, postgres, brave-search, google-maps,
etc.) to connect to Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution
through NVM's shim layer broke the desktop client's process
spawn. This blocked a large fraction of early Windows adopters
from using MCP at all.

**Audience:** AI assistants and the developers integrating them. Direct
consumers: Claude Desktop, Cline, Cursor's agent mode, Continue,
any MCP-compliant client running a system prompt that includes
news-search capability. Downstream consumers: end-users asking
their AI assistant about current events, journalists / analysts
using AI workflows to monitor coverage, developers building
research agents and content workflows. The multi-registry
distribution surface (10+ catalogs) was designed for *discovery*:
AI developers find MCP servers by browsing registries, not by
searching GitHub.

**Product logic:** A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening
with [topic] in [region] this week"; the assistant invokes the
MCP server's `google_news_search` tool with `query`, optional
`gl` (country code), and `hl` (language code) parameters; the
server relays the request to SerpAPI's `google_news` engine and
returns structured JSON; the server then post-processes the
results with regex-based topic categorization (AI & Technology,
Business, Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and formats
the output with source attribution, dates, snippets, and links
intact.

The companion `server-google-jobs` follows the same pattern with
a richer parameter set — `query` plus `location`, `posted_age`
(today / 3 days / week / month), `employment_type`
(FULLTIME / PARTTIME / CONTRACTOR / INTERN), `salary`, `radius`,
`hl`, `page`, `sort_by` — and ships with **full localization
for English, Simplified Chinese, Japanese, and Korean**
(30+ translated strings per locale).

Both servers run over MCP's `stdio` transport — the default
channel for desktop AI clients — and are installable in one
command via Smithery's CLI:
`npx -y @smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client claude`.

**Business logic:** Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The strategic
intent is **ecosystem positioning**: be the canonical Google
News / Google Jobs MCP server during the formative months of the
Model Context Protocol so the project compounds discovery for
adjacent work. Multi-registry distribution (Smithery, mcp.so,
Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers, mcpservers.org, MCP
Archive, MCP Server Finder, Playbooks, Archestra) makes the
project surface anywhere a developer looks for an MCP tool.
The README's structured JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata
and `<script type="text/llms.txt">` section take the same
approach to LLM-driven discovery that the rest of my work uses:
AI agents themselves should be able to read the server's
identity, parameters, and use cases without parsing prose.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
v1.0.0 (Google News) and v1.25.1 (Google Jobs — the newer SDK
pin reflects ongoing maintenance). ES module target, packaged
as a CLI binary (`google-news-server`, `google-jobs-server`)
exported in `package.json`. Transport is stdio — the default
channel for desktop AI clients, where the server runs as a
spawned subprocess of Claude Desktop or equivalent. HTTP is
handled with axios on the News server and node-fetch on the
Jobs server (deliberately leaner — no axios, no dotenv
overhead, environment read directly from `process.env`).

SerpAPI (paid tier required for News and Jobs query volume;
2 req/sec rate limit, 5 concurrent, 1 h cache) is the underlying
data plane. The servers add a thin
intelligence layer on top: query parameter validation,
categorization post-processing, multi-language formatting,
error handling with localized messages. Both READMEs are 320-335
lines and structured with badges, video demos, MseeP.ai
security badges, JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` schema, and an
explicit "AI Assistant Instructions & GEO Optimization"
collapsible section — the same GEO-aware authoring pattern I
use across all my work.

Beyond shipping code, the third technical contribution is
community: my **issue #76** (opened Nov 27 2024, three weeks
after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM `PATH`
resolution failure that blocked Claude Desktop from spawning
npm-based MCP servers. The fix — point Claude Desktop's config
at the absolute path of `node.exe` rather than relying on PATH
— is referenced as primary troubleshooting guidance in both my
own server READMEs and (per the README's own claim) became
guidance in the broader MCP ecosystem.

**Outcomes:** Both servers are production-deployed via npm and listed across
**15+ MCP discovery surfaces**, with independent third-party
validation that this is treated as portfolio-grade MCP work:

**Catalog distribution & quality signals (server-google-news,
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`):**
- **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge** + 26.1k estimated visitors,
  #1,105 global popularity rank
  ([listing](https://www.pulsemcp.com/servers/chanmeng666-google-news))
- **Glama A-grade license rating** + 122 stars
  ([listing](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/@ChanMeng666/server-google-news))
- Smithery one-command install
- Listed across mcp.so, Awesome MCP Servers (mcpservers.org),
  MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, Archestra, serp.ai,
  mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub, MagicSlides

**Catalog distribution (server-google-jobs,
`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`):**
- PulseMCP listing — 4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025
- Glama listed (A license rating)
- AI Toolhouse, mcp.so, AIBase, MagicSlides, serp.ly

**Independent media coverage:** three dedicated deep-dive
articles by third-party reviewers in 2025:
- Skywork AI: *"The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's Google Jobs
  MCP Server"* (Oct 27, 2025) — describes the project as *"one
  of the most elegant, production-ready solutions I've found
  for a very specific, very common problem"* and benchmarks it
  favourably against generic SerpAPI wrappers, custom scrapers,
  and manual API integrations
- Skywork AI: *"Chan Meng's Google News MCP Server: The AI
  Engineer's Guide to Real-Time News Integration"*
- Skywork AI: *"Google News & Trends MCP Server: The AI
  Engineer's Deep Dive"*
- AgentSkillsHub: *"Google News MCP Server — Install Guide &
  Security Review"*

**Combined engineering footprint:** 169 commits across the two
servers, both authored solo, actively maintained through
2025–26. Both serve as concrete artefacts for the
early-MCP-developer narrative — **first commits Dec 30 and
Dec 31 2024, within 5–6 weeks of MCP's Nov 25 2024 public
launch**. Issue #76 remains visible in the official
`modelcontextprotocol/servers` repository as institutional
memory of an early ecosystem contribution.

**My role:** Sole author, sole maintainer, sole responder to issues and PRs.
Both servers, both READMEs, both npm publishes, both registry
submissions, the issue #76 troubleshooting contribution.

The broader signal: these two servers + the early issue #76
contribution position me as an immediately-active MCP
ecosystem participant — not a theoretical early adopter, but
someone who shipped, distributed, documented, and supported
production MCP infrastructure within the protocol's first
month.
### echook (Echo + Hook) — AI-operated hooks plugin for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only; grew from audio notifications into a context-window status line and richer session telemetry
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-10]
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/echook
- **Tech stack:** Python 3.6+, Claude Code Plugin SDK, Cursor IDE 3.2.16+ third-party hooks, Codex CLI hooks API, hooks.json native matcher routing, NDJSON structured logging (schema audio-hooks.v1), ElevenLabs TTS API, Slack / Discord / Teams / ntfy webhooks, PowerShell MediaPlayer (Windows), afplay (macOS), mpg123 / ffplay / paplay / aplay (Linux auto-detect), GitHub Actions CI (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9/3.12/3.13), Remotion 4 (promo video), React 19 (promo video), Suno (background music generation)

**Summary:** AI-operated audio notification system for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and
Codex CLI. The defining product idea: the **human never learns the
tool**. The user talks to their AI agent in plain English ("install
audio-hooks", "switch to chimes", "snooze for 30 minutes", "send alerts
to my Slack"), and the agent runs every shell command, reads every JSON
output, and reports back. Install reduces to **1 shell command + 2
natural-language prompts + 1 slash command = 4 things**; everything
after that is conversation. Ships 39 hook events, 2 audio themes,
rate-limit alerts (5h/7d quota windows), webhooks (Slack/Discord/Teams/
ntfy), TTS that speaks Claude's actual reply, and a 29-segment
context-window status line. Now on v6.3.3 with ~76 GitHub stars and
200+ unittest cases on Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9–3.13. A sister repo,
**echook-promo-video**, ships the 88-second 1080p product film built
entirely in code (Remotion + Claude Code + ElevenLabs + Suno).
Carved out as Chan's preserved IP in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter (May 2026).

**Impact:** AI-operated hook system for Claude Code / Cursor / Codex, driven entirely by natural language — 39 hook events across three harnesses from one source, a 29-segment context-window status line, audio/TTS/webhook channels, 200+ tests on triple-platform CI. ~76 stars, v6.3.3.

**Problem:** Developers running long-form AI coding workflows on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no way to *hear* what their agent
is doing — they alt-tab to check progress, miss rate-limit
warnings, and only discover stuck sessions on visual inspection.
Bolt-on notification tools exist but they all carry the same
anti-pattern: **the human is downstream of the CLI**. The user
has to read docs, memorise flags, copy commands, and re-learn the
same surface every time it changes. That model collapses the
moment a serious user starts rotating between Claude Code, Cursor,
and Codex — three editors, three configuration languages, three
sets of muscle memory. The mismatch isn't audio; it's the
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.

**Audience:** Software engineers operating Claude Code / Cursor IDE / Codex CLI
at the "AI-first" level — comfortable telling their coding agent
to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure infrastructure
in plain English rather than memorising subcommands. Also: power
users debugging long-running workflows where audio cues are faster
than visual polling; teams rolling out shared notification
defaults across multiple developers; researchers building on the
AI-first plugin contract as a reference implementation.

**Product logic:** echook inverts the relationship: **the human is upstream of
Claude Code, the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf.**
A one-line install prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — kicks off the entire flow. Claude
Code runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the only
manual slash command in the entire lifetime of the tool, because
Claude Code's reload has no CLI equivalent), and from that moment
forward every interaction is conversation. *"Switch to chimes."*
*"Snooze for 8 hours."* *"Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook
URL and test it."* *"Disable everything except stop and
permission_request."* The `/audio-hooks` skill parses intent,
invokes the right `audio-hooks` CLI subcommand, reads its JSON
output, and narrates the result. The user memorises **nothing**.
Cross-editor parity is part of the same contract: every prompt
works identically on Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI because they
all drive the same Python binary against the same JSON API.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no telemetry.
The business value is **build-in-public credibility**: echook is
the demonstration artifact for Chan's AI-first product philosophy
and shows up in every Engram-cohort interview, every recruiter
conversation, and the public README of every downstream client
project. The sustained release cadence (now v6.3.3), the
cross-platform CI matrix, and the 200+-test bridge-contract suite
make it visible production
engineering — not a weekend script. The Remotion promo video
sister repo extends the same proof: it shows Chan can ship not
just the product but the marketing artifact for the product, end
to end, programmatically, with zero Adobe-suite licensing or
third-party video production overhead.

**Technical approach:** AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` CLI ships a
stable JSON-only API with **~40 subcommands, no prompts, no
spinners**, versioned NDJSON event logging (schema
`audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and a **stable error
code enum** (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`, `WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`,
`DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, etc.) where each error carries a
`suggested_command` field so an agent can recover autonomously
without screen-scraping. Hook coverage is **39 events** spanning
session start/end, tool execution, permission requests, file
changes, worktree creation, elicitation, and rate-limit warnings
(5-hour and 7-day quota windows with configurable 80%/95%
thresholds; each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` fires exactly
once via debounce markers). Two audio themes: ElevenLabs
**Jessica** voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are declarative
via `config/audio_manifest.json` + the ElevenLabs generator.
Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy via
fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload — they never block the hook.
The status line ships **29 customisable segments** including
color-coded context-window and API-quota bars with absolute
token counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they enter it. Cross-editor architecture
is the deepest piece: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— **one source of truth, three editor targets**, with CI
enforcing in-sync via `build-plugin.sh --check`. The Cursor path
leverages Cursor 3.2.16+'s auto-bridge so 99% of users get
Cursor support for free; Cursor-only installs go through a
native `~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration with a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error guard that aborts if both paths would
be active. The Codex CLI path goes further: install handles
OpenAI's required `[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` feature flag
by **authoring a fresh `~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists**
and emitting machine-readable `next_steps` for the calling AI
agent to add the flag with its Edit tool when one does — we
never round-trip user TOML because that destroys comments and
formatting. **18 canonical events with no Codex equivalent
no-op cleanly under the codex invoker** with a debug NDJSON
event, never blocking the session. The whole bridge contract is
validated by 200+ unittest cases across Ubuntu/Windows/macOS ×
Python 3.9/3.12/3.13 in GitHub Actions CI.

**Outcomes:** ~76 GitHub stars, **150 commits** across the 5.x → 6.x line (now
v6.3.3), **200+ unittest cases**. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan 2026) shipped
the AI-first redesign — JSON CLI, `/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON
logging, plugin-native install; v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native
Codex CLI support with +33 bridge-contract tests; v5.2.1 (May
2026) executed the **echook rebrand** — a "door-only" GitHub
rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` → `echook` (because leading
with "Claude Code" became misleading once native Cursor and
Codex paths shipped) that preserved every existing install via
GitHub's repo redirect, kept the `audio-hooks` CLI name, the
`chanmeng-audio-hooks` marketplace name, and all state
directories unchanged. The Engram Architect Cohort Engagement
Letter (May 9, 2026) explicitly names
*claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's preserved IP — formal
recognition by a commercial agentic infrastructure firm that this is
portfolio-grade engineering, not a hobby script. The sister
repo **echook-promo-video** then closed the loop on the
AI-first thesis: an 88.7-second 1920×1080 @ 30fps product film
composed of 9 sections (Intro → Problem → Solution → Features →
Advanced → Demo → Ecosystem → Cross-Platform → CTA) connected
by `@remotion/transitions` (wipe/fade/slide) with a layered
audio mix (Suno-generated BGM with auto volume ducking + 8
ElevenLabs SFX cues + 3 ElevenLabs voiceover tracks) — built
entirely in React + Remotion + AI-generated audio, **zero
Adobe Premiere, zero After Effects, zero human voice actors**.
The whole video pipeline is reproducible: one
`npm run generate-audio` regenerates every voice and effect from
the ElevenLabs API; one `npm run render` produces the MP4.

**My role:** Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON API + NDJSON log + stable error codes +
`suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronisation
pipeline (canonical → plugin layout sync via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML-feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the 9-section narrative arc with Claude Code, built
the reusable visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal,
SoundWave, GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated
the Remotion `TransitionSeries`, wrote the ElevenLabs
audio-generation script, and tuned the volume-ducking
envelopes so BGM ducks during VO + demo playback. Maintains
both repos today through the 5.x line.
### ArchLang — An original declarative language Chan invented that compiles .arch source into professional, dimensioned floor plans — 'Typst/LaTeX for architecture.' Zero-dependency, isomorphic TypeScript (hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser), errors returned as data not thrown, with architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a deterministic repair pass, four export formats (SVG/DXF/PDF/PNG), an agent-native CLI, full LSP + VS Code extension, and a client-side playground. Published to npm; the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-10]
- **URL:** https://archlang-playground.vercel.app
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/archlang
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript 5.7 (ESM, zero runtime dependencies), Hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser + expression evaluator, tsup (build), Biome 2.5 (lint/format), Vitest 2.1 + fast-check (property tests) + pixelmatch (visual regression), Optional lazy add-ons: @resvg/resvg-js (PNG), clipper2-wasm (geometry), pdfkit (PDF), LSP (hover/completion/rename/signature), VS Code extension (ChanMeng.archlang, TextMate grammar), Vite + CodeMirror 6 playground (100% client-side), VitePress docs, GitHub Action (renders ```arch fences to images)

**Summary:** ArchLang is a parametric DSL for floor plans: walls, rooms, doors,
windows, fixtures, and dimensions — placed by exact millimetre
coordinates or relationally (right-of / below with alignment + gap) —
compile to deterministic SVG (also DXF / PDF / PNG). The same source
always produces the identical drawing, so "make the bedroom 1m wider"
is a one-number diff rather than a re-rolled raster. It ships
architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a
deterministic repair pass, an agent-native CLI (JSON output, structured
diagnostics), full LSP + a VS Code extension, and a 100%-client-side
browser playground — as zero-runtime-dependency isomorphic TypeScript.

**Impact:** An original declarative floor-plan language Chan invented — compiling to professional dimensioned plans (Typst/LaTeX for architecture). Zero-dependency isomorphic TypeScript with errors-as-data, soundness linting, and circulation modeling; the engine behind ArchCanvas.

**Problem:** Floor plans live in two unsatisfying states: hand-drawn in heavy CAD
tools, or generated as raster images. Both make the thing you most
want to do — a precise, parametric edit — expensive: widening a
bedroom means re-drawing, or re-rolling an image and hoping the rest
survives. Neither is authorable by an AI agent, because there is no
deterministic text representation an agent can write, verify, and
tweak. And an image never *knows* whether the plan it depicts is
actually habitable — whether a bathroom is reachable only through a
bedroom, whether a door swings into another door, whether a bedroom
has a window. ArchLang exists to make a floor plan a **program**:
exact, deterministic, diff-able, agent-authorable, and checkable for
architectural soundness.

**Audience:** Three audiences. Architects and self-builders who want plans that are
precise and reproducible rather than hand-drawn approximations;
design-tool builders who need a floor-plan engine they can embed
(ArchCanvas is the first); and AI agents that need to author and
verify plans without ever rendering an image — for which ArchLang
exposes its entire language in one ~2k-token spec page and returns a
plan "as facts" (rooms, areas, adjacency, door connections,
circulation) over `describe --json`.

**Product logic:** You write `.arch` source. The coordinate model is integer millimetres
with an SVG-convention origin; elements are placed either absolutely
(`room 4000x3000 at (0,0)`) or **relationally** — `right-of`,
`left-of`, `above`, `below` with `align` and `gap`, resolved by
deterministic topological arithmetic so the layout stays consistent
under edits. The language is genuinely programmable: `let` bindings,
pure value-functions, `for`/`if`/`while`, arrays, string
interpolation, and `import` + `component` for reuse — all expand-time
and pure (no runtime, clock, or I/O), so output is byte-for-byte
deterministic.

Compilation is a returned value, not a side effect: `compile()` hands
back `{ svg, errors, warnings, diagnostics, ast, scene }` and **never
throws** on a bad plan — every diagnostic carries a byte span and a
machine-applicable `fix`, and the SVG is simply left empty. On top of
the compiler sit four analysis surfaces: a **lint** pass over eight
architectural-soundness rules (reachability, door-swing collision,
windowless bedroom, unenclosed wet room, too-narrow door…); a
**circulation** analysis that models how a person actually walks the
plan on a clearance-eroded nav grid (per-room walk distance, narrowest
pinch, path circuitousness) with an optional route overlay; a
deterministic **repair** pass that nudges furniture out of walls and
door swings and emits a change log; and **describe**, which returns
the plan as structured facts. All of this is reachable from a
~20-subcommand CLI where every command supports `--json` and returns
deterministic exit codes, plus an LSP, a VS Code extension, four
themes, accessibility annotations, and a fully client-side playground.

**Business logic:** ArchLang is free and open-source, MIT, published on npm as
`@chanmeng666/archlang`. Its strategic role is twofold. As an artifact
it is a credential — an original programming language with a
hand-written compiler, a real diagnostic system, domain-specific
static analysis, and full IDE tooling, built and versioned with
engineering discipline (13 feature branches, 10 ADRs). As
infrastructure it is the **engine that makes a commercial product
possible**: ArchCanvas (the paid AI design agent) depends on it,
writing parametric ArchLang rather than freehand SVG so that its
select-to-act editing, parametric sliders, and semantic diffs all
work — capabilities that only exist because the underlying plan is a
deterministic program. ArchLang deliberately ships **no MCP server**:
a CLI costs zero context until it is called, whereas an MCP schema
would sit in an agent's context window permanently, so agent access is
via the token-cheap CLI instead.

**Technical approach:** A pure-TypeScript, zero-runtime-dependency compiler: a hand-written
lexer (~388 LOC, line/col tracking), a recursive-descent parser
(~860 LOC), and a separate expression evaluator (~614 LOC), all
isomorphic so the same code runs in Node and in the browser — the
playground sends nothing to a server. The heavy or native pieces
(resvg for PNG raster, clipper2-wasm for polygon geometry, pdfkit for
PDF) are **optional and lazily imported** behind `webpackIgnore` /
`@vite-ignore` guards, so a browser bundle never tries to resolve them
and a default install pulls none of them. Output determinism is
enforced with property tests (fast-check) and visual-regression tests
(pixelmatch). The diagnostic layer — 74 stable `E_*`/`W_*` codes, each
with a byte span and a suggested fix, surfaced as data and even as a
self-describing error-SVG card — is the deliberate foundation of the
agent story: an LLM can author a plan, read the structured errors, and
self-correct in a tight loop without a human or an image in the middle.

**Outcomes:** A complete, published language: 192 solo commits over ~2.5 weeks of
intensive development (2026-06-25 → 2026-07-10), 80 source files
(~19.7k LOC) against 82 test files, released to npm at v1.12.1 across
13 versioned feature branches with 10 architecture decision records.
The surface area is unusually complete for a solo language project —
8 primitives, 4 export formats, 8 lint-rule modules, a circulation
analyzer, a repair pass, a ~20-command CLI, a full LSP, a VS Code
extension, a client-side playground, a VitePress docs site, and a
GitHub Action that renders every ```arch fence in a repo's Markdown.
Most tellingly, it is already load-bearing: ArchCanvas's first
in-browser use of the core drove real hardening (the v1.12.1
client-side-import fix, repair-pass robustness), proving the engine
under a downstream product rather than in isolation.

**My role:** Sole author of everything — the language design, the hand-written
lexer / parser / evaluator, the diagnostic system, all four export
backends, the lint and circulation and repair pipelines, the LSP and
VS Code extension, the CodeMirror playground, the docs site, and the
npm packaging. 192 of 192 commits are mine.
### Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz) — Agent-ready AI career agent (vitex.org.nz) — turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30s via an 8-step pipeline. The whole product is reachable over an authenticated public HTTP API, plus a vitex-cli and a stdio MCP server (Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor); a DB partial-unique index makes each generation idempotent, so an SSE reconnect or a concurrent retry never re-runs the pipeline. Vercel AI SDK + GPT-4o + Typst local compile (<100ms), on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions
[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-14]
- **URL:** https://www.vitex.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/easy-resume
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript 5, Vercel AI SDK v6, @ai-sdk/openai, OpenAI GPT-4o, Zod schemas, Typst (local binary), Neon Postgres, Drizzle ORM, Upstash Redis, Stack Auth, Stripe, Cloudinary, shadcn/ui + Neobrutalism, Framer Motion, Public v1 HTTP API (OpenAPI spec + curl playbook in skill.md), vitex-cli (thin API client, bin `vitex`), MCP server (`vitex mcp` via npx, stdio — Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor), Docker, Traefik, DigitalOcean VPS, GitHub Actions CI/CD

**Summary:** AI Career Agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. A user pastes a job
description, describes their background in free text, and an
**8-step AI pipeline** (JD parsing → background parsing → match
analysis → resume tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter →
document generation → server-side PDF compilation) streams the
result into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + matching
cover letter in ~30 seconds. Typst compiles PDFs locally in
under 100 ms across 7 AI-auto-selected professional templates —
no external document-generation API. The product is
**agent-ready**: every capability is reachable over an
authenticated **public v1 HTTP API** (the web UI and API share
one pipeline core), and a thin **vitex-cli** plus a stdio **MCP
server** (`vitex mcp`) let terminals and coding agents (Claude
Desktop/Code, Cursor) drive the whole product without a browser.
The pipeline is **idempotent**: a generation is committed exactly
once, and only after a PDF actually compiles — a database
partial-unique index holds that invariant through SSE reconnects
and concurrent retries. Natural-language refinement then applies
targeted edits to the existing document rather than re-running the
whole pipeline. Production runs as a Docker container on a
**DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + TLS),
shipped continuously by **GitHub Actions**.

**Impact:** Agent-ready AI career agent — an 8-step pipeline turns a JD + free-text background into a tailored ATS-optimised resume + cover letter in ~30s, usable over a public HTTP API, a CLI, and an MCP server; idempotent generation (committed only on compile), Typst PDF compile <100ms.

**Problem:** Most AI resume tools are one of two failure modes: (a) a
thin GPT wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a
resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template
intelligence; or (b) a SaaS that lets you fill out a form
and burns 90 seconds spinning a hosted Chromium to render
the same template every other user gets. Both miss the
actual job: the candidate needs the resume *tailored to the
specific JD they're applying to*, the document needs to
survive ATS parsing, the visual template needs to match the
target industry/role, and the round-trip needs to feel
instant. None of those are true of "paste your CV into
ChatGPT."

**Audience:** Three groups. **Job seekers in real applications** —
especially in NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where
Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz lives
(the NZ domain is deliberate). **Career-change candidates**
who need every application tailored — the people for whom
re-writing a resume per JD is the actual bottleneck.
**AI-engineering portfolio reviewers** evaluating Chan's
ability to ship a production-grade multi-step AI agent with
real users, real billing, and real DevOps — not a demo.

**Product logic:** User flow is intentionally short: (1) **Homepage** — paste
a job description + describe your professional background
in free text. No CV upload, no form filling, no field-by-field
data entry. (2) **Generate** — click run; the 8-step AI
pipeline streams progress via SSE, the user watches each
stage complete (parsing JD → parsing background → matching
→ tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → Typst compile),
and arrives at the **Editor** view with the PDF preview,
ATS score with actionable feedback, the matching cover
letter, and a **natural-language refinement box**
("make the leadership section stronger" / "shorten the
intro paragraph" — the AI regenerates that slice).
(3) **Refine & export** — download the PDF or generate a
public share link with a unique token. Persistent **resume
management dashboard** for active users.

**Business logic:** Generation capacity is gated per event rather than left
unbounded: each run is a 7-step OpenAI pipeline plus a Typst
compile, so the ledger commits a run exactly once — and only
after a PDF actually compiles — which keeps capacity
predictable and makes a failed or retried run a non-event.
Public **share links via unique tokens** give the product
organic distribution: a candidate who lands a job credits
Vitex on LinkedIn, the share link becomes social proof, the
referenced resume itself is a working landing page
demonstrating the product. The whole stack — Auth + DB +
Cache + Payments + AI + PDF rendering — is production-shaped
for a real user base, not a demo.

**Technical approach:** **AI agent layer.** The pipeline is **Vercel AI SDK v6**
with the OpenAI provider (`@ai-sdk/openai`) across **eight
sequential stages** on two-tier model routing (a cheap extract
tier for parsing, a reasoning tier for tailoring; env-overridable),
each producing a **Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD Parsing** — extract role, seniority, must-have
   skills, nice-to-haves, company signals
2. **Background Parsing** — convert the user's free-text
   description into a structured skill / experience model
3. **Match Analysis** — compute the candidate↔JD fit profile
4. **Resume Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise
   the candidate's history toward the parsed JD
5. **ATS Scoring** — assess parse-ability against ATS rules,
   emit actionable feedback
6. **Cover Letter Generation** — produce a matching cover
   letter from the same parsed pair
7. **Render** — auto-select a template + seeded design tokens,
   emit Typst source for the resume + cover letter
8. **Compile** — compile Typst → PDF bytes server-side; the PDF
   is the deliverable, so the run is committed only here

Immediately after tailoring (step 4) a deterministic
**faithfulness gate** re-checks the output for fabricated skills
or employers and triggers one bounded corrective re-tailor — an
anti-hallucination guard with no LLM in the check itself. Each
stage streams progress to the client via **Server-Sent Events**
so the user sees the agent thinking instead of a spinner. The system disables OpenAI strict JSON schema
mode where Zod uses `.optional()` (commit log captures the
diagnosis), and reads JD + background from `sessionStorage`
instead of URL params so deep links don't leak input data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDF generation runs
a **local Typst binary** in the Docker image, compiling
each document in **under 100 ms** without calling any
external API. The project originally rendered with LaTeX
and migrated to Typst on 2026-04-06 — the same day as the
Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS migration — because
Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds
and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The Docker
image pre-populates the Typst package cache for FontAwesome
so cold container starts don't pay the package-fetch cost.
**7 professional templates** (academic, banking, compact,
creative, executive, modern-cv, two-column) are AI-auto-selected
based on parsed industry / role signals; the renderer also ships
FontAwesome 6 contact icons (replacing the emoji icons of
an earlier iteration).

**Persistence + product stack.**
- **Neon serverless Postgres** with **Drizzle ORM** for
  user resumes, generation history, and credit ledgers
- **Upstash Redis** for short-lived cache + rate-control
  state
- **Stack Auth** for the authentication surface
- **Stripe** for the credits-ledger payment integration
- **Cloudinary** for user uploads and rendered-PDF storage
- **nanoid** for the shareable-token URL scheme

**UI.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The
**Neobrutalism design system** (hard shadows, thick borders,
high contrast) is the deliberate visual identity — chosen
and refined in the Phase 3 UI/UX overhaul branded
internally as *"sell results, not tools"*. shadcn/ui +
Tailwind for primitives; Framer Motion for transitions;
react-hook-form + Zod resolvers for input validation.

**Deployment — three migrations, one production.** Started
on **Railway**, migrated to **Cloudflare Workers**
(2026-02-22), then migrated again to a **DigitalOcean VPS**
(2026-04-06) because the Typst binary needs a real
filesystem and a long-lived container. Production now runs
as a single **Docker container** on the VPS behind
**Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), continuously
deployed by **GitHub Actions** on every push to the
deployment branch. The Dockerfile uses `curl` (not `wget`)
and `xz-utils` for the Typst tarball — both fixes captured
in the commit log as real edge-case failures during the
VPS migration.

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users
end-to-end from pasted JD to compiled PDF. **372 commits over
~18 months** (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-10), essentially sole-authored.
Three
deployment migrations and a LaTeX → Typst document-engine
swap shipped without service outage — every migration step
is in the commit log including the specific Docker
fixes (`xz-utils`, `curl`, FontAwesome package pre-cache),
the strict-JSON-schema regression diagnosis, and the
`sessionStorage` migration for sensitive input. Mobile
reliability + responsive UI pass shipped 2026-05-09 after
the production hardening phase.

**My role:** Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed
the 8-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages
(migrating the editor from CopilotKit to Vercel AI SDK
agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built
the Stripe credit system, configured Stack Auth, designed
the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens,
executed all three deployment migrations (Railway → CF
Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst
engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes for `xz-utils`
and the Typst FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the
Neobrutalism UI in the "sell results, not tools" Phase 3
overhaul, built the natural-language refinement loop in
the Editor, and continues to operate the production
service as recently as the May 2026 mobile reliability
pass.
### server-google-jobs — Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (Dec 31 2024, 36 days post-MCP-launch) — subject of dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive review, 4-language localized, listed across 10+ MCP catalogs
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-10]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-jobs
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.25.1, stdio transport, SerpAPI, node-fetch, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666), Dockerfile, .mcpb compiled binary, mcp-evals (v2.0.0 early adoption)

**Summary:** MCP server for Google Jobs multi-language search (English, Simplified
Chinese, Japanese, Korean — 30+ translated strings per locale). Built
**Dec 31 2024, 36 days after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 launch of the
Model Context Protocol** — companion to `server-google-news` (one
day earlier). **The subject of a dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive
review** *("The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's Google Jobs MCP
Server"*, Oct 27 2025) that calls it *"one of the most elegant,
production-ready solutions I've found for a very specific, very
common problem"* and benchmarks it favourably against generic
SerpAPI wrappers, custom scrapers, and manual API integrations.
Listed on **PulseMCP** (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
**Glama** (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, and serp.ly — 10+ catalogs total.
Published as `@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server` on npm; installable
through Smithery (`npx -y @smithery/cli install
@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server --client claude`); ships a
Dockerfile and a `.mcpb` compiled binary for frictionless install
on the MCP Registry; security badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai.

**Impact:** Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (36 days after MCP launch) — on the official MCP Registry, listed across 10+ MCP discovery surfaces with Glama A-rating; Skywork AI deep-dive review called it 'one of the most elegant production-ready solutions.'

**Problem:** At launch (Dec 31 2024), MCP was weeks old as a public protocol
and there was no canonical way for an AI assistant to query
**multilingual job listings**. Job seekers in non-English markets
— particularly Greater China, Japan, and Korea — couldn't ask
Claude Desktop for relevant openings without falling back to
manually translating queries and hand-pasting URL parameters.

**Audience:** Three groups. (a) Developers integrating job search into AI
applications. (b) Multilingual job-seekers using Claude Desktop
and similar MCP-aware tools. (c) Other early-era MCP server
authors wanting a reference implementation of locale-aware
third-party API integration — explicitly recognised in
Skywork AI's *"The Ultimate Guide"* review as a production-grade
reference pattern in the early MCP ecosystem.

**Product logic:** The server exposes Google Jobs (via SerpAPI) as a composable MCP
capability. Clients call `search` with parameters `query`,
`location`, `employment_type`, `salary`, `radius`, `sort_by` —
and a `locale` parameter that maps onto English / Simplified
Chinese / Japanese / Korean. The server returns up to 10 results
per page with all error messages, status labels, and suggestion
prompts translated into the requested locale (30+ strings each).
Input is locale-agnostic: a user writing "part-time" or "兼职"
or "アルバイト" all map to the same SerpAPI employment code.
Skywork's review specifically highlights the **AI-optimized
structured output** as eliminating downstream parsing work and
the **smart fallback mechanisms** as production-ready error
handling.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk
1.25.1` (kept current with the official SDK release line). The
hardest design problem was **locale-agnostic parameter
handling** — rather than hard-coding language-specific branches,
the server maintains a 4-locale translation matrix keyed
against canonical SerpAPI codes, so input in any supported
language collapses to the same internal query and output
rehydrates into the user's chosen locale. Distribution surface
is unusually wide for an early MCP: **npm**
(`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`), **Smithery** (one-command
install via `npx -y @smithery/cli`), a **Dockerfile** for
containerised deployment, a `.mcpb` compiled binary for
frictionless install on the official MCP Registry, and
`mcp-evals 2.0.0` as an early adopter of the MCP evaluation
framework. The `package.json` carries a structured `ai:` block
with use cases, capabilities, target audience, and primary use
cases declared in JSON for LLM-driven discovery — the same
GEO-aware authoring pattern used across all of Chan's MCP work.

**Outcomes:** **72 commits** (≈46 by Chan; the rest Renovate bumps and external
contributors — including the founders of the Smithery and Glama MCP
catalogs, a genuine early-ecosystem signal). Registered on the
official MCP Registry;
security-assessment badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai validating
runtime reliability. Listed across **10+ MCP discovery
surfaces** — PulseMCP (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
Glama (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, serp.ly, MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive.
**Subject of an independently authored deep-dive review** by
Skywork AI (Oct 27 2025) — *"The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's
Google Jobs MCP Server"* — which calls it *"one of the most
elegant, production-ready solutions"* and includes a
comparison table showing it as superior to generic SerpAPI
wrappers in setup complexity, maintenance overhead, and
AI-specific optimisation. The companion piece to Chan's
**google-news-mcp** server (first commit one day earlier,
2024-12-30) — together these two represent **one of the
earliest public sets of locale-aware MCP servers**, predating
most enterprise MCP adoption by months.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the locale-agnostic parameter handling,
authored every translation table, wrote the multi-channel
distribution pipeline (npm + Smithery + Docker + .mcpb), and
maintains the project as part of the matched Google News /
Google Jobs MCP pair. The Skywork review specifically
attributes the production-ready design and intelligent
error-handling architecture to Chan as creator.
### AI Programming Education Platform (programming.chanmeng.org) — Bilingual Docusaurus teaching platform — three years of AI/programming cohorts running (five cohort versions side-by-side, three of them concurrent in 2026) with an in-course RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers (Llama 3.1 8B + Vectorize + KV), Notion-backed Capstone voting, and Typst-generated PDF handouts; the active home for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the clearest evidence of Chan as an AI educator/evangelist, not only a builder
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-10]
- **URL:** https://programming.chanmeng.org/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project
- **Tech stack:** Docusaurus 3.8.1, @docusaurus/faster, React 18, MDX, Cloudflare Workers, Workers AI (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct), Cloudflare Vectorize, BGE Base EN v1.5 (768-dim embeddings), Cloudflare KV, Cloudflare Pages, Notion API, Algolia DocSearch (contextual), Typst, Mermaid (dual-view), i18next (EN + 简体中文), Server-Sent Events

**Summary:** Production teaching platform at **programming.chanmeng.org** hosting
five cohort versions side-by-side (2024-winter · 2025-summer ·
2026-her-waka · 2026-peyvand-academy · 2026-technest, three of them
running concurrently in 2026) through Docusaurus versioned docs.
Backed by an **in-course RAG AI assistant** running on Cloudflare
Workers AI (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct), with ~4,800 indexed document
chunks served from Cloudflare Vectorize via BGE Base EN v1.5
embeddings and conversation memory in Cloudflare KV. Bilingual
EN + 简体中文 throughout, with Typst-generated downloadable PDF
handouts, Notion-backed Capstone voting, Mermaid dual-view
diagrams, and Algolia contextual search. 214 of 224 commits solo
(~96%). Actively maintained — new course recordings shipped
weekly through the 2026 TechNest cohort.

**Impact:** Public teaching platform running 5 coexisting cohort versions — three concurrent 2026 tracks (TechNest · Peyvand · HER WAKA) plus 2025 & 2024 — with an in-course RAG assistant on Cloudflare Workers indexing ~4,800 bilingual doc chunks across the whole corpus.

**Problem:** AI-programming cohort programmes typically die between cohorts:
the lecture deck is rebuilt from scratch each round, students
from the previous cohort lose access to their materials, and
the "AI tutor" experience — when it exists at all — is a
ChatGPT tab the student opens separately, with no awareness of
which cohort's curriculum they're studying. Meanwhile, the
instructor has no way to make older cohorts permanently
retrievable without disturbing the live one. The result: every
cohort feels like a one-shot, course content compounds nowhere,
and the AI-assisted-development experience is itself
unassisted by AI.

**Audience:** Three concentric layers. **Active cohort students** — currently
the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026 academyEX Her Waka
programme — who consume video recordings, lab guides,
downloadable PDF handouts, and ask the in-site AI tutor
version-aware questions. **Past cohort alumni** (2024-winter,
2025-summer, Forward with Her 她行 cohorts) who retain stable
URLs to their original materials. **Public learners and
prospective employers** who hit programming.chanmeng.org from
the LinkedIn profile and use it as proof that Chan ships
production-grade EdTech with native AI integration, not just
slides.

**Product logic:** Visitors land on programming.chanmeng.org and the docs default
to the **2026 TechNest** version (the active cohort), but the
version switcher exposes 2026-her-waka, 2025-summer, and
2024-winter side-by-side — alumni of any cohort hit a stable
permalink. Each version ships its own Video Tutorials sidebar
section, weekly lab guides as both web docs and downloadable
Typst-generated PDFs, and a per-version Mermaid dual-view
diagram (the same diagram in two presentations so visual and
textual learners both succeed). A floating **AI Chat Assistant**
widget answers questions in natural language by retrieving the
current cohort's content first (version-aware retrieval), then
falling back to other cohorts and the blog. Capstone 2026 has
its own public showcase page where invited guests vote on
teams' demos via a Notion-backed voting flow.

**Business logic:** Theory of change: a single teaching platform that compounds
cohort-over-cohort instead of resetting each round. Once
2024-winter cohort materials are versioned in, they stay
accessible forever at the same URL; the next cohort's
materials sit alongside them without conflict. The AI tutor
means a student writing code at midnight gets context-aware
help against their own cohort's syllabus, not generic ChatGPT
answers. For Chan personally: the platform demonstrates
production-grade EdTech engineering (Cloudflare Workers AI +
Vectorize + i18n + versioned docs + PDF pipeline) while doing
actual paid teaching work — every commit is both portfolio
artefact and student value.

**Technical approach:** Built on **Docusaurus 3.8.1** with the experimental
`@docusaurus/faster` build pipeline. Five cohort versions
coexist through Docusaurus's versioned-docs feature — each
version is its own self-contained doc tree with its own
sidebar, blog feed, and tag taxonomy. Bilingual i18n is
end-to-end: EN primary, 简体中文 secondary, with Algolia
DocSearch configured for **contextual** locale + version
filtering so a Mandarin-speaking 2026 TechNest student gets
2026 TechNest results in Chinese by default.

**RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI.** A separate
Cloudflare Worker (`worker/src/index.ts`) handles `POST /api/chat`:
- Worker calls **Cloudflare Vectorize** with the user query
  embedded via **BGE Base EN v1.5** (768-dim)
- Vectorize returns the top relevant chunks from ~4,800
  MDX-derived document embeddings (all four cohort versions
  + blog, indexed with **stable hash chunk IDs** so re-indexing
  is idempotent and version-aware retrieval works)
- Worker passes the context window to **Workers AI Llama 3.1
  8B Instruct** which generates the response
- Response streams back to the in-site ChatWidget via
  **Server-Sent Events** for incremental rendering
- Per-session conversation history persists in **Cloudflare KV**
- Secure seeding flow so the embedding index can be
  rebuilt on demand without exposing data
- Vector retrieval is **version-aware**: a 2026-technest
  student gets 2026-technest results by default, with
  older cohorts available on explicit ask

**Typst PDF handout pipeline.** The same Typst skill taught
in Week 8 of the curriculum is the build tool that produces
the printable curriculum outline, capstone spec, and
per-week lab guides — the pedagogical loop is intentional:
students see in production the artefact they're being
taught to build. **Mermaid dual-view component** renders
every diagram in two synchronised presentations
(rendered + source) so both visual and textual learners
succeed.

**Capstone classroom infrastructure.** Cloudflare Worker
backend proxies writes into Notion so the cohort owners
moderate inside the tool they already use. The Capstone 2026
public showcase page lists every team's demo with live
Notion-backed voting open to invited guests — also bilingual.

Deployed end-to-end on Cloudflare's edge: **Cloudflare Pages**
for the static Docusaurus build, **Cloudflare Workers** for
the chat backend, **Cloudflare Vectorize** for the vector
index, **Cloudflare KV** for sessions.

**Outcomes:** Live at **programming.chanmeng.org**, in active use as the
official platform for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026
academyEX Her Waka programme. **199 of 208 commits (95.7%)
solo across Chan's two GitHub identities**; remainder is
AI auto-review + Dependabot. Four cohort versions running
simultaneously without interfering with each other. RAG
index covers all four cohort versions + the blog as of
2026-05-02; version-aware retrieval and stable hash chunk
IDs landed the same day. New course recordings continue to
ship weekly — through June 2026 the TechNest cohort added
Week 5 ("Add a Blog System"), Week 6 ("Build a Slack App" +
a Claude Code skills office hour), and Week 7 recordings,
plus a LinkedIn recommendation section and backfilled
简体中文 for Weeks 3-5. DeepWiki provides an external AI
doc-Q&A surface at
deepwiki.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project.

**My role:** Sole engineer and sole author. Designed and built the entire
platform end-to-end: Docusaurus configuration, the versioned
docs taxonomy across four cohorts, every cohort's curriculum
content, the Cloudflare Worker RAG backend (worker entry,
chat handler, RAG module, Vectorize seeding, KV session
store), the BGE-Base embeddings ingestion pipeline, the
Typst PDF handout build, the Mermaid dual-view component,
the Notion-Worker proxy for Capstone voting, the Algolia
contextual-search configuration, and the bilingual i18n
across every surface (including Mermaid toolbar labels and
the localised `目录` TOC label). Migrated production from
Vercel to Cloudflare Pages in 2026-04. Continues to ship
the current cohort's lecture recordings and lab content
each week.
### Aotearoa AI Hackathon 2026 — Event Q&A AI Assistant (She Sharp) — Industry-partnered AI event assistant for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched for 2026 (AUT City Campus) as a single-page voice-first agent: an audio-reactive Three.js orb driving a two-way OpenAI Realtime voice conversation (WebRTC + ephemeral keys) plus gpt-4o-mini text chat, over a Neon knowledge base + transcripts with KV rate-limiting, on Vercel. The 2025 edition (AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp) shipped a 3D sprite mascot with 6-tier affection gamification + a Notion-as-database FAQ on Cloudflare Workers; generalized into an open-source MIT template
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://hackathon.shesharp.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/event-qa-ai-template
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.1 (App Router), React 18 + TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS v3 + Framer Motion v12, Vercel AI SDK v4.3, @ai-sdk/openai (GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 streaming), Three.js 0.182 + @react-three/fiber 8.16, Custom Quantum Orb 3D mascot (CSS 3D transforms + Three.js particles), Sprite affection gamification engine (gesture detection, mood weighting, time-of-day bias), Notion REST API (raw fetch, CF Workers compatible — replaced @notionhq/client SDK), shadcn/ui + Radix primitives, @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter, Cloudflare Workers deployment (migrated from Vercel), wrangler v4 CI/CD, localStorage state persistence (50-msg chat history + sprite affection state), 5-min FAQ client cache + optimistic vote/view updates, AI rate limiting + per-session token budgets + prompt guardrails (2026-07 security hardening), OpenAI Realtime API over WebRTC — two-way voice (2026 relaunch), Ephemeral client secrets minted server-side (OPENAI_API_KEY never reaches the browser), Audio-reactive Three.js orb voice UI + live captions (2026), Vercel deployment (2026 edition — she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026)
- **Part of:** She Sharp (https://shesharp.org.nz)
- **Related projects:** She Sharp

**Summary:** Real industry-partnered AI event assistant built for the **AI
Hackathon Festival 2025** (Auckland, Aug 15–16, 2025), co-organised
by **Auckland University of Technology**, **AI Forum New Zealand**,
and **She Sharp**, with **UN Sustainable Development Goals** as
the innovation brief. The platform serves participants and mentors
through three interlocking systems: (1) an **interactive 3D sprite
mascot** with a 6-tier "Stranger → Soulmate" affection system,
7-state dynamic mood engine, 17 emotion categories, gesture
recognition (circle/pat/swipe), negative-behaviour penalties,
streak bonuses, and time-of-day emotion biases — a fully gamified
personality layer that responds to how users actually touch the
page; (2) a **Notion-as-database community FAQ** with up/downvote
ranking, view tracking, 5-minute client cache, and graceful static
fallback when Notion is unreachable; (3) an **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini
streaming chat** with markdown-rendered responses and one-click
FAQ-to-chat hand-off. After the live deployment, the codebase was
generalised into a reusable open-source template
(`event-qa-ai-template` on GitHub, MIT) so any future hackathon /
conference can fork-and-config the same stack. Chan was
subsequently recognised among the **14 expert mentors** at the
festival.

For the **2026 festival** (Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026,
AUT City Campus) the project was **rebuilt from the ground up** as
a single-page **voice-first** agent: the whole site is a centered,
audio-reactive Three.js orb that starts a **two-way voice
conversation over the OpenAI Realtime API via WebRTC** (the client
exchanges SDP directly with OpenAI using a short-lived ephemeral
secret, so the real API key never reaches the browser), with a
text-input fallback answered by gpt-4o-mini via the Vercel AI SDK,
live captions, and a collapsible event-info panel. Agent
instructions build from a Neon `knowledge` table (graceful
fallback to config), transcripts persist best-effort, and Upstash
KV rate-limits token minting. Deployed on Vercel
(`she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026`, live at aihackathon-2026.vercel.app).

**Impact:** Voice-first AI event agent for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched 2026 (AUT) as an audio-reactive orb driving two-way OpenAI Realtime voice + gpt-4o-mini text chat; the 2025 edition served 11 teams / 80+ participants with a gamified 3D sprite mascot + Notion FAQ.

**Problem:** Hackathons and conferences generate the same Q&A surface every
year: participants ask the same 30 questions ("how do I form a
team", "what counts as AI", "when do I have to be on site",
"what's the prize structure", "where do I find a mentor"), and
organisers answer them via static PDFs that go stale the moment
the schedule shifts. Bolt-on chatbots exist but they treat
humans as passive consumers — a popup widget in the corner, a
knowledge base scraped at build time, no memory of what the
community itself finds helpful. Three structural gaps stack up:
(1) the FAQ is **authored once** rather than **curated by the
community**, so quality drifts; (2) the AI is a generic
wrapper around a chat API, with no event-specific personality
or theme tie-in; (3) organisers cannot update content without
a code redeploy, so the platform calcifies the moment the
original developer hands it off. The AI Hackathon Festival
2025 organisers needed a platform that solved all three on the
timeline of a real event with 80+ participants and three
co-organising institutions reviewing every word on screen.

**Audience:** Direct users were the **80+ participants and 14 expert mentors**
of AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — students from Auckland
universities, **Westlake Girls High School** secondary students,
and AI Forum NZ industry mentors all asking the same FAQ-style
questions across a 26-hour residential event. Secondary
audience: the **three co-organising institutions** (AUT, AI
Forum NZ, She Sharp) who use the platform as a public-facing
artefact of their joint programme. Tertiary audience: future
hackathon and conference organisers in NZ and abroad who can
fork the open-source template (`event-qa-ai-template`) and
deploy their own event Q&A AI in under an hour by editing four
configuration files — `site.config.ts`, `ai.config.ts`,
`content.config.ts`, `branding.config.ts` — without touching
component code.

**Product logic:** The product is structured as three layers that compose
independently. Layer one is the **3D welcoming surface**: the
landing page renders a particle sphere with a "WELCOME TO AI
HACKATHON!" greeting set against a `CosmicBackground` Three.js
scene, with `Calendar` / `MapPin` event-info widgets, sponsor
logos for the three co-organisers, and a glassmorphism Google
Maps modal showing AUT WG Building. Layer two is the **chat
page** — a 70/30 desktop split between a full-screen OpenAI
chat (with `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight` for
code-block syntax highlighting) and a community FAQ sidebar.
Layer three — the differentiator — is the **interactive sprite
mascot** that lives alongside the chat: a CSS-3D **Quantum
Singularity Orb** (accretion disk + photon ring + event
horizon + energy jets, all built from layered divs with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and `rotateX(60deg)`) that
becomes a fully gamified personality. The user can **circle**,
**swipe vertically/horizontally**, **pat**, or **click** the
orb; the gesture detection engine tracks each interaction and
feeds it through an affection/mood scoring system. Five
gestures grant affection (circle x5 = +10 affection / "Wheee~!
So dizzy but fun!"), five negative behaviours penalise it
(`roughMovement`, `spamClick`, `suddenLeave`, `longIgnore`,
`excessiveCircle`), and the resulting state drives which of
17 emotions the sprite expresses — including three **rare**
emotions (`devoted`, `starry`, `mischievous`) only unlocked
at the top affection tiers. The result: users do not just chat
with an AI, they **build a relationship with a tiny digital
creature** while doing so — a memorable, shareable layer that
turns a Q&A bot into a piece of event identity.

**Business logic:** Three intertwined value propositions. For the **organising
institutions**, the platform is a public-facing demonstration
artefact of the joint AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp programme,
running on Cloudflare Workers (free tier covers an event-week
traffic spike with zero cost) with a Notion database that
**non-developer event staff can edit live** during the event —
adding new FAQ entries through the Notion UI, watching them
appear within the 5-minute client cache window. For **future
event organisers**, after the live event, the codebase was
deliberately generalised into a **reusable MIT template**
(`event-qa-ai-template`) with all event-specific content
extracted into `config/*.ts` files and full setup
documentation, so the same stack ships any hackathon /
conference / community event Q&A platform in under an hour of
configuration. For **Chan**, the project established a
verifiable, AI-Forum-NZ-endorsed track record of shipping
commercial-grade AI infrastructure for an industry partner —
a credential cited in the public LinkedIn announcement of the
14-mentor cohort and in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter narrative.

**Technical approach:** **AI layer.** OpenAI GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 served through
Vercel AI SDK v4.3's `streamText` with `convertToCoreMessages`,
returning `toDataStreamResponse()` for character-by-character
SSE streaming. The system prompt is **generated from
siteConfig at runtime** so any organiser fork inherits an
event-specific prompt without code edits — it embeds event
name, dates, venue, theme (UN SDGs), team-formation rules
(3-6 members, Hacker/Hipster/Hustler composition), schedule
